[…] molti […] primi attori furono costretti a lasciare le compagnie inoperose nelle più svariate città d’Italia, prendere il treno, e mettersi a rapporto — come diceva il comunicato — col nuovo ministro. Data l’urgenza del messaggio tutti pensammo che finalmente fosse affrontato e magari risolto il problema del repertorio straniero, la cui proibizione era per la vita del teatro un limite soffocante. Poiché — in quella meravigliosa isola che è il teatro — in quel giugno '43, fra le bombe, i disagi quasi insormontabili in cui si svolgeva il nostro lavoro, tutti erano occupati a pensare al repertorio del prossimo anno, alla formazione delle compagnie; tutti gli attori italiani e io fra loro. Per cui accaldati, variopinti negli abiti estivi, ci ritrovammo riuniti un pomeriggio assolato, nella vasta sala del Ministero di via Veneto […] Nelle prime file delle sedie destinate agli attori, Dina Galli sedeva impeccabile lanciando in giro sguardi burloni. Emma Gramatica più lontana, sorrideva a qualcuno modesta e malinconica. Ruggeri, quasi nascosto in un angolo, sfuggiva qualunque commento, la bellissima testa d’avorio piegata in una rassegnazione superba. Cimata, squisito, salutava ogni tanto qualcuno scuotendo amabile la testa. Solo Ricci era rimasto a Milano. C’era Cervi bonario ancora in piedi; stava parlando con Gandusio seduto che sogghignava piacevolmente. […] Finì. Come Dio volle, finì. Ci ritrovammo fra i platani di via Veneto che già la sera consolava con la sua frescura. Quelli che erano venuti da fuori, lasciando le compagnie inoperose, i teatri chiusi in lontane città, non fecero commenti. E chi altro avrebbe dunque potuto farne?

Mi dondolavo una sera d’estate, sospesa fra pensieri apparentemente privi di logica. Ricordo che il mio profumo si sposava splendidamente con quello notturno. Nella mia mente sfilavano senza coerenza idee, ricordi, parole, avvenimenti. Lasciai che affiorassero liberamente frammenti della mia vita, parentesi scelte anche dall’immaginario: emozioni. Spingendosi chiassosi e disordinati, all’improvviso entrarono nel bosco che abito personaggi, fatti, fate, invenzioni e bugie: quelle che mi porto dentro solitamente mi apparvero circondandomi… Fu così che pensai di accoglierli “Da me” tutti assieme per una notte…

The real paradox is that even Giuliana [Fiastri], Carlo's first wife [Ponti], wanted her freedom and, as a lawyer, studied the situation in search of a way out. Three times the bridges asked for the cancellation to the Sacra Rota, and three times it was denied them. The only solution to take away from us if nothing else from Bigamia's pity seemed to be the cancellation of the Mexican marriage, which however represented more a step back than a step forward. I suffered a lot, but in the bottom of my heart I had a clean consciousness and, however shocked by all the public and private accusations that rained on us, I knew I was right. I felt married, and this was enough for me, even if it was not always easy to live in the pillory, stamped with infamy. On the doors of the churches they were posted by the posters that put my films to the index and at the same time invited the faithful to pray for our slicing souls. We were submerged with often fierce letters, the worst of which came from a group of women of Pozzuoli. Feeling attacked by my city, from the heart of my childhood, of my land, caused me a deep wound, from which I started to recover a lot. Italy was divided between who was with us and who was against us. It was the Italy of Coppi and the White Lady, Rossellini and Bergman, a bigoted Italy that did not have a long future. Ten years more, and everything would have resolved with the referendum on divorce. But then we were still far away and we left only to suffer without making us annihilate.

Quanto finora ho detto per il teatro potrei cominciare a dirlo anche per la cinematografia. Fino a un certo punto vedendomi costantemente tenuta fuori da ogni tentativo di esperimento della mia figura sullo schermo, io stessa mi domandai: «Ma forse in film io non verrò bene? ». Sapevo che in tutti gli altri paesi dopo l ’avvento del film parlato, la maggior parte delle attrici e degli attori del teatro di prosa erano stati chiamati a interpretare i nuovi soggetti. I took the bus every morning to go to the new school, this time I publish. I met Catherine Deneuve at the stop. She is bigger than me a few years old. While turning into the future woman, you are perfectly aware of what is beautiful already physically and what are our defects. I looked at myself in all mirrors. I looked at myself in the eyes of others. I looked at me more in the eyes of other women than in those of men. For shyness and instinct; I already knew that man's eyes are a deforming mirror, even if I was still not familiar with the opposite sex. And then I didn't have the audacity that would allow me to look at a man in the eye. I was just looking for an answer that concerned me selfishly. I remember very well our scrutiny each other, as if we had intuited the mutual future, as if we were recognized; Two people belonging, even if in a very different way, to the same family, linked by a similar destiny. There was dismay in our looks. It was not a question of establishing who was the highest, who was the slimmer, who had bigger eyes, the thicker hair, the thinner ankles, how we dress, how we behaved. Each studied the other way of climbing the bus. We didn't go to the same school. We spied naively. The mirror, the mirror of Eric, always comes to mind. The mirror in Anthonny's way of being, my cousin. I was the mirror of Catherine, she was mine, and each through the other looked at herself. We did not express feelings towards each other, our gaze was only in search of an answer that concerned ourselves. Ours was a relationship of anxious, trepidant expectation. Each was waiting for the other, but not the other for the other; He waited for the other for himself. I didn't know her was also called Catherine.

The film stuntman of the film have saved me more than once. I resisted heroically. Fortunately I was very well on horseback and I was not afraid. That actor had ended up in the United States, on the front page of the crime news, for throwing his girlfriend from the fourth floor of a hotel, fracturing it. Fortunately, the girl did not die and he got away with little. It was truly terrifying, gigantic, a wardrobe, hateful, arrogant, without any charm. My room was on the thirtieth floor of the hotel. I was really unfortunate. One night I felt knocking, it was him. I already slept; He screamed: "I'm Brown." I with the small voice, small behind the door, I said I was sleeping. And he: "Open immediately or throw the door down." He screamed and beat his fists against the door. They could not even hear on the plans below. Meanwhile, I said to myself: "The stronger it knocks, the better, so they will come out to help me." Nothing ... I opened. I looked at it. I understood that I was not afraid, even if it wasn't exactly like that; I was asking my fear. My quiet attitude, my look at him straight in the eyes, my ostentatious detachment made him back away. That night I had learned two things: don't expect help from others and once again having more confidence in me. The next day the stuntman loosened his saddle of this purpose and, at the time of climbing on horseback, made a great rough.

In the face of this war, I [...] remained to fight next to Luigi Pirandello, then always exposed to all the revenge of the opponents, feeling that the cause for which it was fought was holy, and all in favor of us actors for the dignity of ours profession and for the honor of Italian theater. From the contracts that companies made with the Compagnia del Teatro d'Arte in Rome, directed by Luigi Pirandello, it was demanded that alongside the name of the Maestro it would imagine as with the diction of writing, unique and only, mine; [...] A company that in all the nations of Europe had been welcomed with all the honors of the highest theaters, was condemned to turn almost only in the province [...] the only hope of salvation, South America. La sera l’oscuramento ci apparve come diverso, più buio e insidioso. Roma emerse, in quella notte senza luna, bianca e immensa, come un gregge rifugiato sotto la mestizia delle sue cupole. La gente cominciò a muoversi piena di sospettosa incertezza in quella oscurità innaturale, fra le luci d’acquario che illuminavano gli autobus. […] A Roma cominciò così la gran commedia degli allarmi, delle paure incontrollate, e della curiosa assuefazione a quella vita da trincea fatta in vestaglia da camera. Gli attici furono abbandonati e le belle terrazze romane andarono deserte; tutti si rifugiavano nelle cantine alla luce avventurosa delle lampade tascabili, sotto l’impero dei capistabile, che guidavano quella turba di gente impaurita con durezza dispotica.

Nella notte volle buttarsi nel cortile, nella vasca dove guazzavano i cani. Aprì la finestra. Fra la nebbia, vedeva i lumi girare come trottole impazzite. Scovato dalla polizia nella stessa notte che la Croce Rossa portava sua moglie al pronto soccorso, vi doveva scontare una pena di sette mesi.

Mi sono qualche volta interrogata sull'indipendenza del destino. Credo che nessuno sia più bisognoso di aiuto di lui. Quando è solo fa quelle cose fatali quanto banali che lo hanno reso famoso, la tegola in testa, la voragine sotto i piedi. Sono i suoi gesti rabbiosi che lo rendono temibile ai poveri di spirito. Le sue trame sono all'impronta della viltà. Ci sono degli eletti che individua e prende di mira, approfittando della loro indifferenza. Che io sappia, il destino non va mai perso di vista.

Quante donne violentate sono state trovate uccise nei prati!» […] Di suo padre sapeva l’integrità dei principi morali, la severità del carattere e la facilità con la quale cedeva agli scatti di ira: per sua madre temeva l’immenso, doloroso stupore che la rivelazione le avrebbe procurato. Il ricordo di «quella sera» divenne il suo segreto, la ragione della sua paura, la causa della sua angoscia.

I went up to mine, directed to the airport. Marcello launched me a very long look, perhaps guessing that we would never have seen each other again. A Bologna, come tutti sanno, vive il più mistico e lirico fra i pittori contemporanei. Morandi. Egli dipinge nella sua stessa camera da letto; su di un tavolo le sue bottiglie, assorte, attendono che egli tragga da esse e dagli spazi che le separano, la musica di serena mestizia in cui appariranno nei quadri luminosi e composti. Alla finestra una tenda rigida posta come una vela, dosa nelle varie ore del giorno il riflesso di luce concesso agli oggetti modello sul tavolo fra cui, spesso, è la vita di qualche fiore. In terra, col gesso, sono disegnate le impronte dei piedi del maestro, di modo che, se deve spostarsi, può ritrovare il punto esatto di luce e di prospettiva. Tutto qui, l’universo di questo grande artista. Per dipingere i suoi paesaggi, traeva invece ispirazione durante la consueta villeggiatura estiva, da un paesino modesto, non lontano da Bologna, di nome Grizzana. […] Appena Sandrino [Bonaccossi] fu a Bologna, cercò naturalmente Morandi, per rendergli omaggio. Il pittore lo ricevette nella sua stanza di vita e di lavoro, fra la polvere delle bottiglie, dinoccolato, fanciullesco, pieno di timida, festosa gentilezza. Si era accorto pochissimo di quanto era avvenuto a Bologna in tutto quel tempo. Era, invece, molto preoccupato per ciò che poteva essere successo a Grizzana. […]

Gli uomini della troupe, devo dire, non dimostrarono di essere idonei agli onori e agli oneri del clima imperiale.Appena sbarcati presero a lamentarsi della polvere che si mescolava ai loro ruscelletti di sudore, cercarono di proteggersi i capelli incollati alle tempie con caschi coloniali e per il timore di serpenti e scorpioni si armarono di stivaloni dentro i quali i loro piedi bollivano. Parevano le caricature del colono disadattato, del borghese in pantofole sbalestrato in Eritrea. Avrebbero dato piena soddisfazione ai detrattori dell’Italia fascista,Io invece, con i miei sandaletti da spiaggia tipo Capri, un fazzoletto di cotone in testa e i miei vestiti a pois, stavo benissimo. All’Asmara poi, dove l’aria era più fine, mi sentivo meravigliosamente e constatavo laverità di quanto avevo letto sulla «Domenica del Corriere»; la nostra Asmara era un giardino. […] Alloggiammo per dieci giorni in un albergo della catena Ciao. Ciao, parola italianissima diventata mondiale  proprio come fascismo  stava anche a indicare la cordialità con la quale trattavamo i nostri buoni indigeni. Cordialità, ricambiata: n’ebbi la prova durante la cena nella residenza del governatore dove insieme a noi furono invitati alcuni notabili eritrei, persone degnissime, che conoscevano Roma, parlavano italiano e sapevano stare a tavola come noi. Avevamo insegnato qualcosa e, al momento del brindisi, dissi con vanto sincero che anche noi attori stavamo facendo qualcosa per l’impero e per il duce. […]

The first effects of war were also felt in the cinema. I was recalled in Rome. He telegraphic the "Celio" who returned immediately. The return journey was rather tiring. Although Italy had declared its neutrality, the vampa of the huge conflict touched her. On the trains, in particular, the restlessness, the discomfort was evident. Travelers appeared worried: the anxiety of the present, the uncertainty of the future, was read in their faces. The speeches were all around the war. Once in Rome, I immediately went to the factory, where I was welcomed with relief by the staff and the actors, who were waiting for me. Everyone was worried about the fact that the "Cines", on which the "Celiofilm" depended, had placed personal and half -paying actors, citing the war as a pretext. In our sector there were the first unpleasant consequences of the European conflict. The amazement and irritation for that arbitrary measure can be good for it. The artists were indignant and everyone was around me hoping that I could fix our situation.

He died during the night, at one, and I knew him from the coffers of a radio. I slipped to the ground affected by pain and guilt. Because that day I did not go up to embrace him? Why didn't I know how to bring him some joy? Fifty -year -olds had passed since his first entrance to the scale in 1917; For the extreme greeting, the teacher instrumentalists wanted to interpret the same funeral march that Victor had directed to Toscanini's funeral, but I did not want to replace another conductor because nobody could reach his height. They said: "We play the teacher for the teacher", and the whole orchestra of the scale snatched the soul of who was there to listen to them.

«Arrivano, arrivano i partigiani!» […] era un corteo numeroso di uomini barbuti, magri, armati di fucili, pistole, mitragliette; alcuni feriti, bendati; le facce dure, determinate, ma che ancora riuscivano a sorridere alla folla festante […] Fra le lacrime accoglievamo quegli uomini che significavano la libertà, la fine del dolore. Non fu proprio così. Era arrivato il tempo delle punizioni […] però era comunque l’inizio della fine della guerra. […] Le ragazze che avevano avuto relazioni con i soldati tedeschi o con i fascisti venivano prelevate dalle loro case e trascinate in strada; qui, in mezzo a una folla urlante, venivano rasate a zero, poi, con il capo cosparso di pece, venivano ricoperte di penne d’oca e costrette fra calci e spintoni a fare il giro del paese in mezzo a gente che le insultava in ogni modo. […] Mia madre si salvò da questa umiliante parata perché fu prelevata a casa da un gruppo di partigiani e poliziotti per essere processata.

I was a little obscured by Franco's personality, but being in the shadows didn't cost me much. My husband fascinated me and I liked to talk about him. So I didn't tell a lot about myself, because what he was talking about seemed more important to me. During the meetings with journalists I rarely opened my mouth, I let Franco do it, also because I liked what he said very much. He was always ironic, did not take himself seriously and purposed with much simplicity. He captured his way of being, even on the set, where he didn't take himself seriously and purposed very simply. [...] He was the man and the protagonist, and he was always at the center of attention. But I, just like the women of the Middle East, spoke little and often remained silent for a long time. However, I was very lively and dynamic, a side of my character that binds to the vitality of Greek women, just as I like to trace my appearance back to the Italian roots. This mixture was fascinating for those who came to interview us and pushed them to discover some of my hidden sides, which was apart from my relationship with Franco.

Una notte vennero in fascisti […] temevano che scoprissero i tre ebrei che che ospitavamo. […] c’era un ragazzo che appena ci vide disse: “Oh! la signora Silvi e il signor Scarabello”. […] era il fattorino della Minerva Film […] per quella volta ci andò bene.

La mia mamma così eroica, lei che non aveva temuto le mine camminandoci in mezzo, che aveva combattuto la fame; lei sopravvissuta i bombardamenti, alle mitragliate, lei che aveva soccorso le sue bambine e sua madre da ogni pericolo, lei che cantava «Ohi May, Ohi Mary» ridendo del desiderio che suscitava negli uomini; lei dalle sottovesti di nylon e dalle braccia profumate di rosa, lei grigia in viso e con gli occhi invasi dal terrore, si allontanava dalla sua casa, dalla sua famiglia, con un branco di lupi. Erano quelli gli eroi? […] Cominciai a capire che le verità erano tante, ognuna diversa, ognuna adatta al fabbisogno del momento. […] In guerra non esistono buoni e cattivi, vinti e vincitori, se ne esce tutti sconfitti perché la gente smarrisce la propria umanità.

Alla stazione di Lubiana trovammo ad attenderci solennemente tutti i rappresentanti del fascio là installato. Fummo anche informati sulla calcolata opportunità del nostro arrivo in quella città e del significato squisitamente nazionalimperialistico che avrebbe assunto […] la nostra recita: quel giorno infatti, si festeggiava a Lubiana l’anniversario della conquista da parte dell’Italia. […] Percorrevamo ora la città nelle automobili dei gerarchi. L’architettura appariva graziosa, ordinata, composta per la maggior parte di quel moderno gotico austriaco a noi già familiare nell’architettura dell’Alto Adige. […] Il teatro si annunciava […] gremito. All’aprirsi del sipario avemmo però la sensazione di uno scherzo o di un errore: il pubblico era composto esclusivamente di persone in divisa militare e, precisamente, di ufficiali italiani, che seguirono religiosamente lo spettacolo, applaudendo alla fine, con entusiasmo assolutamente sproporzionato. […] I nostri ospiti [ufficiali italiani] ci parlavano ancora dei pericoli cui erano continuamente esposti in quella insostenibile situazione di occupanti. Quel giorno stesso, raccontavano, la bandiera italiana, esposta su di un alto campanile per l’anniversario della annessione della città all’Italia era stata misteriosamente tolta e sostituita con quella jugoslava. […] Quel giorno, certo, l’impero fascista festeggiava, anche a Roma, l’anniversario di Lubiana italiana.

Alle otto apre le finestre dello studio, spazza, spolvera, tutto deve essere in ordine per le nove. Un sostituto dell’avvocato le insegna come si adopera il telefono e le detta interminabili «comparse»: la macchina da scrivere le fa male alle spalle, alle reni. […] la prima esperienza di dattilografa non le procurò che amare sorprese. […] Quanta malinconia e rimpianto per le pause del mezzogiorno nel cortile della fabbrica, in compagnia delle amiche!

Perfino la presenza di Trilussa […] non aveva il potere di sollevarmi; ripensavo, anzi, a tutte le volte che in osterie simili a quella, egli aveva recitato quei suoi versi pieni di pacata saggezza, di allusioni e ammonimenti; ricordavo le sue mani immense, delicate, che ne commentavano la dizione con gesti minimi, riunendo l’indice e il pollice per separarli, poi, a esprimere una conclusione logica e amara. Ora quelle grandi mani giacevano sulla tovaglia macchiata di vino, inerti, infelici: anche il suo viso pareva impietrito.

Anche se mia mamma mi ripeteva sempre di non fare la vittima, io mi sentivo una vittima a tutti gli effetti. Percepivo sempre questo senso di ingiustizia, lo stesso che sentii quando Fiore [Argento] mi mostrò la sua cameretta rosa. Era un dato di fatto, mamma non aveva mai alzato le mani su Anna [Ceroli], Fiore non era mai stata abbandonata da papà, e adesso aveva pure il gatto. Anche Anna soffrì per la dipartita di papà. Lei adorava mio padre perché il suo se n’era sempre fregato di lei. Ancora oggi mi dispiace per Anna, senza pace, trascurata e abbandonata da tutti gli uomini della sua vita.

Arrivai a Roma con mia madre e corsi subito al Teatro Odescalchi per prendere il mio posto. La Compagnia era già riunita da qualche tempo; io […] arrivavo ultima. Un uomo calvo e con una piccola barba a punta, nell’oscurità del palcoscenico, il viso pallido e solcato, era balzato in piedi con una agilità portentosa, in contrasto col suo aspetto, appena qualcuno disse il mio nome. E una mano calda e vigorosa scosse più e più volte la mia, con quella cordialità che è propria soltanto agli uomini che sanno dare e fare il bene. […] Curiosità, interesse, invidie, lotte, si erano accese fin da principio; lotte, intendo dire, da parte di chi è stato sempre contrario a ogni nobile e disinteressata impresa d’arte nel mondo del teatro. L’attesa della mia presentazione, acuita dal ritardo, era vivissima. Come per ogni grande impresa ai primi inizi, il primo assestamento era difficile. […] Fu un trionfo. Un trionfo per Bontempelli, per Luigi Pirandello, per il Teatro e per me. Piangevo di consolazione e di beatitudine. Mai più, dopo, ebbi una così piena e pura gioia.

[Visconti] mi strinse le mani fra le sue. Una stretta avvolgente che mi emozionò. « Ah, la nostra piccola Maria», disse con quella voce speciale, irta di erre. Era bello. […] Credo di essermi innamorata subito di lui, contro ogni ragionevolezza. […] Il mio incanto per Visconti mi portò, fatalmente, a fare la conoscenza di […] un uomo il cui nome, pur a distanza di tempo, resta sinonimo d’infamia: Pietro Koch, il cacciatore di partigiani, il torturatore della Repubblica di Salò. Nella mia memoria il volto dell’uno resta indissolubilmente legato a quello dell’altro, come due facce di una medaglia. Mi ricordano amore, tradimento, vergogna, dolore.

Ero decisa a infischiarmi delle convenienze dettate dalla mia famiglia e dalle consuetudini e a gradire l’attenzione di uomini maturi (il signor Solvay, l’onorevole Glori) purché la loro intrusione nella mia vita privata favorisse le mie fantasie di successo; […] Cercavo di ravvisare il mio destino per esclusioni. Ormai certa di «colpire», toccava a me scegliere i bersagli.

I was rocking one summer evening, suspended between thoughts apparently without logic. I remember that my perfume married wonderfully with the night. In my mind, ideas, memories, words, events were paraded without consistency. I let me emerge freely fragments of my life, parentheses also chosen by the imagination: emotions. Pushing himself rowdy and disordered, suddenly they entered the woods that I live characters, facts, fairies, inventions and lies: those that I carry inside usually appeared to me surrounding me ... it was so that I thought I was welcoming "from me" all together for one night ...

Then swear, swear we'll get married. That sentence he uttered with such a precipitation that I was amazed. I turned my head away from him. I sat down on a stone and began to speak quietly to him: "Is it true that everything is allowed to you poets: even to marry and divorce several times? He looked at me with amazement, unbelieving: "And what is the point of this speech now? I was never married! - Yeah, I know. But don't you understand that in Italy we are talking about war? Nino Osilia has already been recalled, and if there is a conflict they will also call you. And away, Fausto, it's absurd to talk about marriage at these times. Don't you think? Then I took a joking tone: sure tactics to overcome the male sadness: "You see, Fausto, "I told him in a cheerful tone "If a war really should break out, I would follow you everywhere: at the front, in the trenches, without fear of anything... " But I don't understand you... you're always in contradiction with yourself. "I approached him, I spilled my head on his shoulder, to shipwreck with my gaze in the depths of the sky.

Federico was my first true love and with him I reached orgasm, which had never happened to me and which consecrated him as the first real love. I was 16. [...] Federico remained at my side at every moment during the shooting of Placido's film. He was proud of me. When the film came out, he went to see him alone and told me that at the end of the screening he was moved and stood up to beat his hands and that the rest of the audience in the room was forced to imitate him. A few months later, my father called me to make a film with him in America. Trauma was the first time my dad and I worked together and I had a fucking fear of letting him down. It was terrible to have to separate myself from Frederick for months.

Io sono morta molte volte in scena. Per esempio in Questa sera si recita a soggetto, quando ero nella compagnia di Vittorio Gassman, negli anni Sessanta. Mommina, il mio personaggio, moriva alla fine di un lungo monologo. Ogni volta che passavo a miglior vita, ricevevo un grande applauso. […] Gassman era un attore pazzesco, oltre che un uomo straordinariamente bello e carismatico. Quando non ero sul palcoscenico insieme a lui, lo guardavo dalle quinte. Aveva una fissazione: portare il teatro nelle fabbriche . Voleva far conoscere i grandi classici agli operai. Così ci obbligava a indossare una tuta e a rappresentare opere come Adelchi e Otello. […] Ho incontrato anche un altro Vittorio: Vittorio De Sica. Ho recitato diretta da lui nel 1973 in Una breve vacanza, da una sceneggiatura di Cesare Zavattini. De Sica mi affidò un ruolo splendido, la signora Scanziani, che mi valse il Nastro d’Argento e la grolla d’Oro come migliore attrice.

Life has put me in the need to do, rather than thinking. And once I started to "do", I had to go on this path: years and years spent going from one job to another, from one plane to another, from one film to another. In these conditions, devoting to the deepening of culture and thought has been almost impossible. They are full of respect and admiration for women who have managed to cultivate thought, and not just action. I have esteem and respect, for example, for Catherine Spaak. Respect for his choices, for his work. The strength of women who fascinate me is also, or above all, the strength of their thoughts.

Ho sempre sentito come un marchio di grande povertà non avere idee, iniziative, amori, passioni. Non ho mai potuto vivere senza innamorarmi del concreto e dell’effimero. Ricordo il mio primo amore; impazzii per una camicetta. L’indossava un ragazzino, un ragazzino brutto; aveva già la pancetta ed era tutto molle. Io che odio le cose mollicce! Eppure aveva una camicetta di struggente bellezza... Era una camicetta a quadri e fiori, verde, blu e turchese, con qualcosa di un rosa impercettibile. Inseguivo la camicetta come Merlino l’incantatore la pietra del Graal attraverso boschi e foreste, e correvo a perdifiato dietro a quel mostrillo grassottello a quadretti incantevoli; volevo toccarli quei quadretti, poter guardare quella camicetta senza affanno: perdermi nella sua bellezza.

Of course, staying in front of the camera was starting to like me, I had soon understood that it would be my best ally, however I detached myself too ease from my characters, letting the emotions assaulted me with waves, intense but short. For me, cinema was still a job like the others. It is Germi's film that served me from revelation. Pietro did not like to speak, he had a shady character, he expressed himself in a grumpy way, he had to grasp his suggestions on the fly. Still, he knew how to make me understand that you could not arrive on the set with your hands in your pocket and wait, just obeying the director. You had to give yourself, let yourself go. That is to say letting yourself be investing, transforming, slavishing from one's role. And at the price of this abandonment that the actor gives life to a character. This new investment perhaps saved me from the tests I accumulated: there was no place for personal concerns on the set. An actor must be emotionally available. It cannot be subscribed to the problems, from its personal history. "Virgins" must be achieved and accept the adventure: let yourself be guided by the director on an unknown land, without fearing the danger. Photogenia was no longer enough for me. But I knew how to listen and I liked to work. Based on these two qualities I built a career that I had not chosen.

«Ho capito che la mia scontentezza veniva da qualcosa che dovevo cercare dentro di me... io volevo diventare qualcuno... fare qualcosa di importante... l’ambizione che inconsciamente mi tormentava, incominciava a dare forma ai miei sogni: violinista... aviatrice... scultrice... non sapevo, non potevo in quel momento sapere. […] Capivo soltanto che dovevo studiare, studiare molto per imparare». In un Oratorio di Porta Ticinese, dei preti, per una cifra veramente irrisoria, davano dei corsi serali nei quali, fra le altre materie, insegnavano anche stenografia e dattilografia. Non fu cosa facile convincere mamma Ernestina a lasciare uscire la figlia di sera; alla fine, stanca di sentire il tono implorante di Rosa e soprattutto convinta che sarebbe stato utile per l’avvenire della figlia acconsentì.

Cristaldi had been a lawyer before becoming a producer. Dozens of pages organized my professional life, fixed the amount of my monthly salary, determined preliminarily and regardless of the importance of my cachet collected by the producers. Pages that established the names of the tailors who dressed myself, the expenses that Vides was charged, the following composed of a driver, secretary, press officer. My life was controlled. Step by step. With those articles, those paragraphs, those chapters on which I had placed my signature, I had sold my soul. The pact forced me to give up my free will. I was no longer entitled to my image. The Vides had bought me as a goods: color and length of the hair, measures, everything was written without me being able to affix even the smallest modification. Every decision concerning my personal life had to be taken in agreement with my producers. Cristaldi was good and generous, but it never failed to remind me that he had created me and that consequently I belonged to him. Even today, many years after our separation, many years after his death, I have not forgotten anything.

My first true love was for a guy in Camargue, starring in the movie Crin blanc. He was a savage, always riding a horse, working in a bull and horse farm. He was elusive and beautiful: a blond gypsy, the first kiss, his, disappointed me. Even today, I don't like kissing on my mouth. I am not convinced of the approach to that intimacy, the way, perhaps too easy, too fast of its achievement. It would be necessary for the mouth to be stripped, to offer half undesired... It would be necessary for the mouth to be always hidden so that the kiss and its nakedness would become a pure gift; an act of secret love, like that other one... Even when I made love the first time I was disappointed. I thought, "It was wrong. This is not the way to do it. "There is a Vanoni song called "It's all here": it tells of a woman who is always surprised, incredulous in the face of the things of life that inevitably disappoint her. There is a mistake, I thought, it cannot be so, in the sense so insignificant, compared to what I had imagined.
Sono stata io a ritirarmi. Mi sono dovuta dedicare ai miei genitori. […] Non potevo prendere impegni. Ho rinunciato a tutto pur di curare mio padre e mia madre.

“Da me” vorrei un giullare, un nano sapiente e folgorante, un antidoto alla malinconia, un elemento che spezzi la riflessione dopo averla provocata, sciolga i nodi in subbuglio; rompa il magnetico potere dell’incertezza. So chi vorrei. Penso al film Un anno vissuto pericolosamente. Lo porterei “Da me” questo film, lo proietterei spesso nelle sere di temporale: non sbiadirebbero le emozioni. Io non voglio sapere che “lui” è una donna, voglio pensare che Linda Hunt è un uomo. Anzi mi piace molto di più sapere che può essere tutti e due. Ecco, luilei, quel personaggio mi richiama al meglio di me. Potrei amarlo, darmi a lui. Il suo aspetto non conta più, per quanto è potente la sua profonda umanità, la sua passionalità, il suo carisma: trascende l’intrascendibile. Mi piacerebbe incontrarlo davvero, vivere l’emozione che mi suscita nel dormiveglia. Tirando i fili delle sue marionette indonesiane, ombre sagomate, il nano sussurra: “La forza della luce e delle tenebre in continua lotta... Tutto è offuscato dal desiderio, dal possesso, come lo specchio dalla polvere...”

[Visconti] revealed to me his intention to pass the lines, to reach the allies, to go back to Italy at their side. [...] Could I guard the family jewels during his absence and take care of the villa in Via Salaria, making sure that everything proceeded as if he were in Rome? I immediately said yes, with some pride, because it seemed to me to be a sign of particular affection and confidence in me. He gave me the keys to the house and the ability to withdraw money from his bank: they could have served him or some of his companions [...] Today, after so many years, I wonder why precisely to me that assignment, which turned out to be so dangerous. [...] The first rule, in hiding, is not to openly involve your family. Visconti couldn't risk his sister Uberta. So he turned to me.

L’Italia si avviava al boom economico: il 31 dicembre 1959, stappammo lo spumante dei mitici anni Sessanta stringendo un contratto settennale con la Vides, casa di produzione cinematografica retta da un torinese di nome Franco Cristaldi. Anche Renato [Mambor] aveva partecipato alla selezione fatta in tutta Italia dai talentscout: sfilammo in un teatro romano davanti a una giuria, con le foto sotto il braccio, ricordo lo sguardo corrucciato di Luchino Visconti. Vennero presi dieci ragazzi, cinque maschi e cinque femmine: ero tra queste.

I remember the promenade on a sunny day. It was dotted with rubble of houses standing in half and inhabited by poor people, children with black skin and narrow yellow curls. The mothers had oxygenated their hair to hide its origins, or rather those of their father, some African American soldier. But how beautiful the sea shining, lapping the golden sand, the breeze that caressed my face. [...] Meanwhile, the famous family golds came to an end [...]. Sometimes my mother, like most of them, was prostitutes to the American military.

I presented myself, all cheated up, to the address that Ponti had given me and found myself in front of a command of the carabinieri. My arms fell: the instinctive distrust, which perhaps instilled my own mommy, immediately made me think of a bad joke. There he is, he's teasing me. That makes the producer like I do the dancer!I felt mounting anger mixed with a bitter sense of humiliation. How could I believe him? Stupid fool!The thought ran immediately to my father, and to the pussies with which he had lured Romilda into his spies. Thank God, though, stories don't always have to repeat the same, and mine was still to be written. The young Carabiniere thought of it to reassure me. If you're looking for the production house PontiDe Laurentiis, it's the door next door.I felt silly and childish, I thanked with a big smile and I got ready to enter the heart of Italian cinema. I'd have turned 17 in a few days.

The director of a male college wrote to me begging me not to send any more photos. Not to answer the demands. Miss Pampanini, you said, these guys can't hold up anymore. On the bedside table, instead of Jesus and Mary, they hold her. The kids have to study, they can't waste their time thinking about these little things... you, Miss Silvana...

L’avvocato Lo Savio accennò alla difficoltà di trovare attrici e ragazze disposte a cimentarsi nel cinematografo e che risultassero bene fotograficamente. Non era stato ancora coniato il termine di « fotogenìa », di cui si è fatto tanto abuso dopo. Io, timidamente, azzardai: «E lei, avvocato, crede che io sia adatta per fare del cinematografo? » Lo Savio si accigliò e rispose: « Signorina, non solo lo credo, ma ne sono certo. Altrimenti non sarei qui stasera. Le pare? »

La signorina Caterina, la sarta, non ha una vera sartoria: in un locale del suo appartamentino ha messo la macchina da cucire, un armadio, un grande specchio e due lavoranti: apprendiste perché costano meno. Quando mi presento, piaccio alla padrona ed entro subito in simpatia con le ragazze. Dopo scuola, invece di ritornare a casa, corro dalla sarta, raccatto gli spilli, levo i fili delle imbastiture, infilo gli aghi, vado in cucina a prendere l’acqua per chi ha sete e porto i vestiti finiti a domicilio: faccio la « piccinina » insomma.

Although I had made my debut at 16 and I was now a worn-out actress, I felt like a beginner. Luchino's presence intimidated me. The idea of that role so interesting but quite far from my personality, made me feel clumsy, insecure. I trusted Girotti with my state of mind. "Don't worry," he said kindly, "I'll give you a pastry that will make you feel great!" It was a real mess. It was a sympamine, a stimulant to which, I would have discovered, I am very sensitive. I felt like an electric current, a nervousness! As a clumsy person, I became feverish. I was overreacting, usually so measured, and this alas brought down the evidence. [...] is, together with that of not being able to make theater, one of my strong regrets. [...] Visconti on the set was hard. Terrible. He treated everyone very badly: many actors cried with him. But he managed to achieve extraordinary results from them, real metamorphosis.

Malgrado l’amore che nutrono per noi, penso che i genitori esercitino anche un controllo, e che verso i tredici o quattrodici anni occorra disfarsene, ricercare l’indipendenza. Più tardi, tra i trenta e i quarantacinque anni, un altro processo si mette in moto. Periodo di costruzione, sia sul piano professionale sia familiare. Infine, verso i quarantacinque anni, quando la maschera della bellezza giovanile svanisce, quando questo demone che ci è stato donato dalla natura non ci protegge più, il sé profondo, vero, emerge. Il ricorso alla fisicità diventa impossibile. Bisogna confrontarsi, una volta per tutte, con il tempo e con la morte. È una questione di scelta, insomma. È il momento in cui si comincia a pensare alla chirurgia estetica per sfuggire alle leggi dell’invecchiamento. Oppure in cui si ha il coraggio di sabotare gli schemi rassicuranti. Tentare di conservare la curiosità dell’adolescenza su un viso adulto. Per niente facile...

Una donna che ama un artista, chissà per quale motivo, si sente parte in causa nelle sue opere. Sono ipocrita nel dire ‘chissà perché’: basta guardare i quadri di Picasso, le trasformazioni che subivano le donne da lui amate, come le ritraeva quando ne era innamorato, e come le ‘scomponeva’ quando il rapporto entrava in crisi. Sì, parte in causa: musa o ispiratrice sono definizioni enfatiche, ma, certo la connessione tra arte e sentimenti, non potevo dissimulare una certa ansia che mi metteva l’evoluzione della pittura di Renato. Tutta lesa a controllare il suo mondo però, ignoravo le mie pulsioni, le mie curiosità, paurosa di perdere terreno nel cuore del pittore, non attribuivo importanza ai miei mutamenti, che erano scosse telluriche: aggrappata a lui come ad una boa, leggevo biografie di artisti alla ricerca di almeno una, compagna o moglie, che ce l’avesse fatta a stare tutta la vita accanto a un pittore. Scoraggiante: le vite degli artisti, labirinti di passioni e tradimenti. Povera me, perduta in un chimerico sogno di unione eterna e sacrale.

Here I am again walking the goddamn Via Veio, the way of dreams and hopes often lost with his sampietrini. [...] I was struggling on those sampietrini, and I felt the annoying danger of losing a heel, but I was too distracted to take seriously, and it happened. Half of the very high heel of paint stuck in a despondency: through the veiled sock I burned my heel on the hot stones. Half a heel stood in the crack. [...] Next to the sidewalk stood a beautiful white car with driver. I recognized the director Goffredo Alessandrini, already then famous though little more than thirty years old, personal friend of Vittorio Mussolini. He went down, took me to the arm, kindly wanted to keep my shoe broken and ordered the driver to take me home. Piazza di Spagna sparkled under a sun that seemed African to me.

Cristaldi had been a lawyer before he became a producer. Dozens of pages organized my professional life, fixed the amount of my monthly salary, determined beforehand and regardless of the importance of my cache collected by the producers. Pages that laid down the names of the tailors who dressed me, the expenses that Vides took on, the following composed of driver, secretary, press officer. My life was controlled. Step by step. With those articles, those paragraphs, those chapters on which I had signed, I had sold my soul. The deal forced me to give up my free will. I had no right to my image anymore. The Vides had bought me as a commodity: color and length of the hair, measurements, everything was written without me being able to make even the smallest modification. Every decision about my personal life had to be made in agreement with my producers. Cristaldi was good and generous, but he never failed to remind me that he had created me and that consequently I belonged to him. Even today, many years after our separation, many years after his death, I have not forgotten anything.

[...] At this I thought as I saw myself as a nigger on the screen [...] Open-scene applause began, in the dark. When the lights turned on, it seemed to me that the room turned against me: everyone turned their backs on the screen to chant Doris Duranti curled up there at the end. [...] There was no critic who found the film less than wonderful, but only the American Gloria Wander [...] he gave me the compliment I expected: "White or black, you are the sexiest woman in Italy."

Le dive cominciarono a ricevere dai combattenti, cartoline militari a migliaia che richiedevano fotografie con autografo da tenere sotto la tenda, da portare in battaglia. E le attrici si prodigarono a firmare migliaia di ritratti da cui sorridevano i loro visi con le labbra, gli occhi e i capelli illuminati lusinghevolmente. […] Fu forse questa intensa richiesta di autografi alle dive da parte dei combattenti, che fece sorgere a qualcuno, lì al Ministero della Cultura Popolare, l’idea brillante delle visite domenicali obbligatorie delle dive agli ospedali militari, riprese dal FilmLuce. Ci era già toccato infatti di recarci al Celio e ad altri due ospedali in seguito a ordini perentori.

What I've said so far for the theater I could start to say for cinematography too. Up to a certain point seeing me constantly kept out of every experiment attempt of my figure on the screen, I myself asked myself: "But maybe in the movie I won't be okay? It's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay. I knew that in all other countries after the advent of the film, most actresses and actors in the theatre of prose had been called to interpret the new subjects. [...] the doubt about my movie possibilities, gradually began to take root in me so much, that only as an essay or trial to myself, I allowed myself to accept a side part in a film of German origin of which we wanted to make the Italian reproduction. That's when someone else's distrust had been reduced to me.

Davanti alla macchina fotografica Rosa doveva esprimere la sfacciataggine della scugnizza, il languore dell’innamorata (« Come l’edera sei tu, tu non morrai se mi vedrai morire » era scritto su una cartolina dove Rosa con occhi languidi appariva in svenevole atteggiamento aggrappata a dei rami di edera) oppure mentre sfogliava una margherita che aveva come stelo un punto interrogativo rovesciato.

I was born in a tumultuous way, in advance of the times, to the Regina Elena clinic where my mother, pregnant in the eighth month, had been urgently taken after falling. It seems that someone, perhaps my brother, had made it stumbled by causing a premature birth. [...] The doctor who gave me born, Dr. Acerbi, in seeing me exclaimed: "Uh, what a Muster of a tusa!", What a child's monster! Thus, at least, I was told. And that feeling of discomfort, the same one that my parents must have tried, I remember it. My first years of life are wrapped in a vague darkness. I crawled around the house and around me I saw threats everywhere. [...] I traveled the floors in the certainty of being in constant danger. Nobody spoke to me. But sometimes I felt adults talk about me as if I weren't there. They said: "Too bad the girl is like that ...". And they didn't finish the sentence. I also didn't speak much. I was so shy that at Christmas I had to recite the poems behind a door. I did not feel any gratification in showing me. I imagined I was adopted, not to be part of that family with which, for dark reasons, I found myself living.

I would like to have a large room, a room that I don't see, that I will never have to see and that nobody will ever have to see. More than a room is a place of memory, dark secret. It is the seat of voluptuousness. The place of games, of eroticism without bridle. It must be, first of all, a game, then, perhaps, can become something else. I know neither the shapes nor the colors of this room; Maybe there is, maybe not. And unluogononluoga. It is perhaps. And I want to continue not to see, but to hear. I want to have my eyes closed on a veil of fresh and light fabric that is also beautiful for those who do not see ... I want to be dressed in white and walk underground; I don't want to know what I wear nor what others wear, don't even imagine it; I will only warn the transparency with the tip of the fingers and, full of this feeling, I will feel good inside. I will touch something fresh under the feet. I know that there are the others near, men and women; They too in the absolute inability to see. Just feel, breathe. I don't know if this place has windows from which the sun could enter, or if it is completely dark. I think I would like more if it were dark, wrapped in a mauve, reassuring. A liquid, fresh and restful darkness. The nocturnal darkness of a moon -less summer night, without crickets. Then, with your hands, feel the others: touch an arm, with one foot touching another foot. Understand if I hear a man or woman, but not know who is absolutely. Imagine how it could be, or how I would like it to be, and decide whether or not to continue the approach according to the elements that I can capture. A dry scent of limoncello, the silky grain of the skin, just barely lukewarm fingers, breath of hay with a short breath ... it must be a cheerful tension, without fear of silence. It cannot be nervous. The wait will not have to know of sin, nor prohibited. Just a game that was born to become the altar of secret desire.

In the first months of 1968, I was desperate enough to fall in love with the first who passed, but not prepared to immediately receive what was happening. At the beginning the movement of the students seemed to me a goliarded, something already seen. [...] But the strength of the events was such, not to leave time to reflect. The reflection would come later, in the meantime, open eyes, ears tense and if ever, reading Marcuse. "Take your wishes for cultural reality," wrote the boys, on the walls of the Sorbonne. That embarrassing season was underway where the rules were turned upside down, mandatory to rethink totally: a mass philosophical movement. In Pesaro I remember, at a Film Festival, a group of students recognized me, and they came to offer me their autographs: I still keep those scribbled signatures.

Tutti venivano a bussare. Tranne i tecnici. Quelli non si azzardavano. Se qualcuno osava di più, beccava certi ceffoni... E quando usciva con la guancia rossa e gli chiedevano che aveva fatto... Ho cercato de bacia’ Silvana... Ma co’ quella non ce sta niente da fare. C’è chi ha fatto carriera andando con qualcuno. Io ho fatto carriera senza bisogno di andare con nessuno. La Magnani aveva visto giusto! E sono molto orgogliosa.

While I started relaxing, I let myself be brought an innocent joke. Or maybe not .. «Cary sent me a bouquet of yellow roses before I left. Yellow jealousy? How cute it is ... "Carlo turned off and printed me a slap, under everyone's eyes. In any case, the white imprint of its fingertips on my red cheek burned with anger and shame. I felt the tears go down one by one on the face. I felt I died, but inside me I knew that somehow I was looking for it. And I don't think about it. At twenty years old he must learn to live, and Cary's love had given me a lot. Perhaps also the courage to fight for a normal life with Carlo. On the other hand, I was young but not stupid. I sensed that that slap, today as today difficult to understand, was the gesture of a man in love, who had seen himself threatened by another man, had risked getting lost and only now was he recovering from fear, sorrow. I cried but not too much because the airplane was full. The hostess approached asking me timidly if I needed something.

Buñuel, who was one of the most acclaimed directors in the world had a reputation to be very demanding. Just a few days before my arrival he had chased an actress away and I was very worried, also because he didn't know me at all. [...] The last thing I wanted was to be removed from the set! Rather, it would have been better to face his judgment Face to you, without witnesses. So I called his assistant and I explained to him that I had urgently speaking to the teacher. I stripped me and, naked as I was, I put on a waterproof of Valentino lined with fur. When Buñuel entered, did he just have time to pronounce the bonjour and the ça va? Of the ritual that I, opening my luxurious trench coat, showed me how a maniac in the bushes of a park does. "Voilà, je suis comme ça." "Mais Madame, Qu'estceque Vous Fites?" He exclaimed him amazed, retreating to small leaps. Then I explained to him that I wanted me to see me before I started and he reassured me, not without a certain embarrassment. [...] During the filming he placed the camera right under the piano. Meanwhile, he did nothing but repeat: "Madame, Je n suis pas a pornographe, vous savez", Madam, I'm not a pornographer, know?

Ancora oggi, se ci penso, rivivo la terribile gelosia che mi colse appena fummo sulla spiaggia e […] vidi [Clara Calamai] muoversi in costume da bagno: Clara era già la femme fatale che di lì a poco sarebbe esplosa in film come La cena delle beffe e pensavo fosse il massimo che un uomo potesse desiderare. Io sentivo di valere meno, come una di quelle ragazze disgraziate che impersonavo sullo schermo.

Of my childhood I remember very confused facts and situations, but of a particular I have clear memory. I constantly kept a long bruise at the top of the forehead, just below the attacking of the hair. The cause of that sign was sleep: every evening I followed the company to recite small and large roles, daughters of kings, bracious children, protagonists of pitiful stories ... The fact is that it touched me to stay awake late. Often, as soon as I could, as I already told, I lying inside a costume case and I was doing a nap. More than once my mother kept me asleep in her arms as she returned with the fool home. But when in the morning they woke me up because I prepared me to go to school, I was in a debit of sleep to the point that, going to the bathroom, dangling, I sat on the toilet and after a moment, inevitably, I fell forward with my head beating on the edge of the sink. For this I brought the bangs: to hide the bruise.

1954 had been a year full of changes, surprises, rapid and irreversible growths. I had met De Sica and Marcello [Mastroianni], I had been directed by Blasetti and Soldati, I had sung and recited with great masters, I had gone from comedian to tragic without losing my identity and had consolidated my relationship with Carlo. I was becoming a star, and the world was starting to talk about me. In the spring I had been for the first time in Cannes to present Carosello Neapolitan, the only great film by Ettore Giannini, considered the only Italian musical able to compete with those Americans, in which I had done a small part singing, dubbed, "O surdato" (O surdato) '.In June I had also been in Berlin, where a famous photo portrays me alongside Gina Lollobrigida, along with Yvonne De Carlo. In October, on my return from Comacchio, it was the turn of London, where I was invited to the Italian Film Festival.

Un po’ per civetteria, un po’ per comprensibile abbassamento della vista, ho chiesto al curatore di questo libro di leggermi ad alta voce ciò che ha scritto sulla base dei miei sconocchiati appunti. Così, come in una commedia di Pirandello, mi sono vista rappresentata; ma al contrario dei personaggi di Pirandello ho riconosciuto appieno la Doris Duranti che volevo essere, contro tutti e tutto. È davanti a questa Doris, io che non ricordo di essermi mai commossa per la sorte di una persona, mi sono sentita la voce legata, gli occhi gonfi e per dire col solito tono: «Bene, anche questa è finita»,ho dovuto vuotare il bicchiere di rum.

During the shooting, Carlo called me to give me some bad news. I was at the hotel. We had just finished filming. "Sophia..." "What is it?" Why is that voice? It's... it's Marilyn's death, it's now known. Barbiturates. They talk about suicide.I remained silent, hung on the phone, without knowing what to say, so much so that Charles got restless. He knew well that behind that rigorous and determined face of mine there was an emotional woman hiding. "Sophia, are you there? "Yes, yes, of course, where do you want me to go... "That so early, so ambiguous death troubled me in the depths and made me think a lot. I let myself be invaded by a thousand swirling and confused images. I thought about the sense of beauty, the loneliness, the need to feel loved that is in the heart of each of us. Relive that seductive, veiled smile of nostalgia. It's not enough to be the most beautiful woman in the world to be happy. Marilyn was a great actress, crushed by the weight of her talent, by men who had asked her for everything without giving anything in return, or who had pretended to transform it according to their tastes. She couldn't find her way. I felt a thrill, as if a shadow rested all around. Ours is a cruel world, which feeds itself, is content with appearance and rarely wonders what's underneath it. That's why it's up to us to keep the fairy tale anchored in life, not to lie to ourselves who we are, where we come from. Marilyn's charm ended up destroying her by reducing her to a joyless sex Symbol.

Roma si delineò come meta precisa. Le circostanze, però, imposero che raggiungessi la capitale con itinerario da gambero, tant’è vero che mi ritrovai a Milano. Per tentare la carriera cinematografica dovevo rompere con la famiglia. Per rompere con la famiglia mi conveniva trovare un appoggio consistente, anzi una catapulta che mi proiettasse fuori da Livorno. […] Avevo un abito celeste a grandi pois bianchi e un fiore nei capelli neri. […] Presi un taxi e subito un treno per Milano. Addio al passato, senza rimpianti. Sfilavano davanti al finestrino le rocce della costa, le pinete marittime, e non avvertivo alcuna nostalgia. Io stessa ero meravigliata della serenità con la quale fuggivo di casa, esperienza che tante giovani hanno fatto ma, ne sono sicura, sempre con un brivido d’angoscia. Non io.

Alle undici del mattino, seduta sul lettone dei miei genitori lato papà, mentre vagabondavo tra i canali della televisione più grande di tutta la casa, arrivò Lei. Non potevo credere che qualcosa del genere potesse attirare così tanto l’attenzione di tutto quello che avevo dentro, in fondo era soltanto la scena di un film che nonostante mi abbia suggerito chi fossi veramente, il titolo non l’ho mai scoperto e mai cercato.

Andai da sola in un ospedale militare […] Un ragazzo, operato per una grave ferita […], era semicosciente ma trovò comunque la forza per ringraziarmi della visita […] L’onore era mio per aver avuto modo di dare qualche minuto di serenità a lui che era stato al fronte per difendere la mia stessa patria. I medici […] dissero che c’erano poche speranze di guarigione. Il mattino dopo telefonai per avere notizie e il medico mi disse: «Signora, è successo un miracolo, il ragazzo sta migliorando». […] in un altro ospedale, sempre a Roma, […] vidi un sommergibilista completamente paralizzato. […] Incontrai un ragazzo a cui avevano amputato tutte e due le gambe e dopo qualche settimana mi venne incontro camminando felice. Gli avevano applicato due arti artificiali.

Sembro incinta al settimo mese e la cosa non mi dispiace affatto, mi porta in uno stato catartico che mi impone di continuare a recitare la parte della gravida felice. Mi purifico dalla colpa e sogno che tutto quel cibo pari a uno scaffale intero di un vecchio alimentari sia il mio primo figlio e che ad aspettarmi fuori dalla porta ci sia tutto l’amore del mondo. Mi siedo e mi rialzo sentendo la pressione della pancia, fingo di percepire qualcuno dentro e per un attimo mi dona quella pienezza che da sola proprio non riesco a sentire.

It was during the filming of a particular day that Riccardo Scicolone died. One morning, my sister telephoned me on the set, in tears: "Sofì ', come immediately, dad is sick." I ran to the hospital and around his bed I found the women of his life. Mammina, Maria, her last German companion. I approached and walked my hand. He stared at me. And I supported his gaze, as paralyzed. I smiled at him, then I moved away to the window where Maria was crying. I looked out. Seen from above, machines, passers -by, bicycles seemed anything but toys. I tried to cry too but I was not able to.

I arrived in Rome with my mother and immediately ran to the Odescalchi Theater to take my place. The company had already gathered for some time; I [...] I came last. A bald man and with a small pointed beard, in the darkness of the stage, his pale and furrowed face, had jumped up with a portentous agility, in contrast with his appearance, as soon as someone said my name. And a warm and vigorous hand shook mine over and over again, with that cordiality that is proper to men who know how to give and do good. [...] Curiosity, interest, envy, struggles, had turned on from the beginning; Lotte, I mean, by those who have always been against every noble and disinterested art business in the world of theater. The expectation of my presentation, exacerbated by the delay, was very lively. As with every great enterprise in the first beginning, the first settlement was difficult. [...] It was a triumph. A triumph for Bontempelli, for Luigi Pirandello, for the theater and for me. I cried of consolation and bliss. Never again, afterwards, I had such a full and pure joy.

My father had two passions: the watches, which disassembled and reassembled with meticulousness, and the violin, which played to make us fall asleep. I grew up in a family of musicians: my sister practiced piano and singing, Adrien with guitar. The big exit of the family was for the concert. But no movie theater! Of American films, I knew the posters well: how not to notice the faces of men and women chained in a kiss... yet, at fifteen years old, neither I nor my sister, younger than a year, were allowed to attend shows contrary to morale. We could dream in front of the pictures posted on the panels at the entrance of the halls. But we girls didn't have to know anything about loving life. And let's forget about the "sexual" life, a word we wouldn't have said without blushing. And not even the simple functioning of our bodies. In those days, at school, we studied the reproduction of amoebas... and I remember the panic that struck Blanche [his sister] at her first menstruation, the upset cry that called my father to help, and the slap that earned her that scare! At our house it was a ritual, a quick way to teach daughters not to talk about those things there. Every month I heard Mom tell Dad, with much mysterious, that the British had landed. A no-girl who used to put my brothers on the windows... I was squealing. I didn't do it...

At a certain point in my career I rebelled against this naive image "water and soap", because everything has its time and a change takes place when you least expect it. I realized that I could no longer be what was piloted and put there as if it were a puppet. I needed to establish myself as a person and no longer as the one who should be guided by parents before and by a husband later. I began to have my say and it was a trauma in the family, because Franco did not know me from the aspect of reactive and autonomous woman, but I could no longer and I had to change, in any way.

You're right. I apologize if I appeared bored or annoyed and if I was abrupt with all those who pointed it out. I really look like Ingrid Bergman. Really. So... what do I do? What do you want from me? Having admitted the resemblance, I have the impression that you expect me to do something, but what? You want me to say something? What? As a daughter to them, I could look like my mom or dad, I think I should say I was lucky to have taken it from her. And... tell me, isn't it normal to look like a parent? You want me to agree with that woman who wrote to Vogue, after reading an article about me: "Isabella Rossellini has the presumption that her career is the result of her own talent, rather than of her extraordinary resemblance to her mother." Ow! That kind of claim hurts! But is that all? Am I just the rough copy of Ingrid Bergman? Some kind of imposture?

Ho girato due film con Vittorio Gassman. Quando l’ho conosciuto era l’amante della mia amica Annette Strøyberg. Appena arrivata in Italia vedevo sui rotocalchi le foto scattate in via Veneto delle stars dell’epoca. La dolce vita di Fellini sotto gli ombrelloni del Cafè de Paris con Anita Ekberg e tanti altri attori famosi assorti davanti a bicchieri di liquori colorati: sembravano felici. Mi ricordo le fotografie di Annette e Gassman scattate in via Veneto; com’erano giovani! Annette aveva piedi mozzafiato; calzava sempre scarpe con il tacco basso molto scollate; si intravedeva l’ini zio delle prime due dita del piede; sembrava l’attaccatura dei seni esposti da una generosa scollatura. Ero affascinata da questo simbolo erotico. Quando parlavo con lei non staccavo mai lo sguardo dai suoi piedi.

Mi presentai alla porta di Cinecittà, custodita dal ferocissimo portiere Pappalardo che, con estrema severità e durezza, sbarrava il passo agli sconosciuti. Ma la mia caparbietà, unita a disarmanti sorriso, conquistò il cerbero portiere e riuscii a entrare per la prima volta nel fatato mondo del cinema: si avverava il mio sogno più grande! Camminare lungo i viali del mondo della celluloide, incontrare qua e là attori noti, vedere i set dove si giravano i film, mi emozionò tantissimo, ma nonostante la mia eccitazione mi sentivo sicura e già appartenente a questo mondo tanto desiderato […] mi sentivo come “Alice nel paese delle meraviglie”.

I saw that he was going out of the tub; I turned to the other side.  Dry me in the back. I did it badly.  You want to sleep with me tonight? I was gonna tremble. I was gonna hit her. There was a long silence.  You're stupid, he said, you can go. I left.

Ogni tanto succede a me e Dario di sfogliare pubblicazioni illustrate da foto sul nostro lavoro e quasi all’unisono esclamiamo: «Abbiamo fatto cose da pazzi! Ma dove trovavamo il tempo per dormire?!» In mezzo a tanta baraonda di spettacoli, partecipazione a dibattiti e manifestazioni, interventi nelle fabbriche occupate e nelle università, rappresentazioni all’estero, c’è da chiederci com’è che non si sia esplosi come i palloncini di Carnevale troppo pompati. A ogni modo, non c’era né festa né periodo di vacanza che ci facesse prender fiato: dagli anni Settanta agli anni Ottanta non erava mo dentro una vita, ma piuttosto in una specie di giostra con finale da montagne russe. Oltre a mettere in scena questi spettacoli di satira politica, personalmente io recitavo testi sul tema delle donne in chiave quasi sempre spassosa e altre volte drammatica; Dario continuava a raccogliere e scrivere testi tratti dalla Bibbia, dagli scritti eretici, dai Vangeli apocrifi: il Mistero buffo ormai era recitato a puntate.

A un certo punto della mia vita mi sono accorta che il lavoro di attrice non mi faceva impazzire, che non lo amavo poi così tanto. La gente non ci crede, pensano che siano bugie. Invece è vero, non avrei mai scelto questa professione, che richiede un tipo di carattere che io non ho: presenzialismo, arroganza e buoni gomiti. Quando mi sono accorta che non amavo questo mestiere, era troppo tardi, ero già sposata con Dario e avevamo la nostra compagnia. Se non fossi stata indirizzata a questa professione dalla mia famiglia, mi sarei impegnata nel sociale, avrei potuto anche essere un avvocato o un medico.

After just six months of engagement [...] on August 28, 1940 we were married. It was the wedding of the year because we were two famous characters, in fact my husband, in addition to having made the Serie A championships, was also gold medal at the Berlin Olympics in 1936. [...] A great crowd was waiting for us outside the church. To see their kindnesses, they have been waiting for about an hour. [...] For cinematic needs, my hair was blond. Gigi wanted me for the ceremony with my natural color, black raven. My simple, white and long dress, I think, had been packaged by the Fontana sisters. [...] A few days before the wedding, Nazzari called me and asked me if I would prefer, as a wedding gift, a refrigerator, a radio or a jewel. [...] I didn't have my home yet, so I immediately opted for a jewel. [...] he asked me how many movies I had made until then. I honestly said five.  [...] I got home a bracelet with five [...] rows of tears. Nazzari's generosity was such that if I had said that the movies [...] had been thirty, I would have arrived a bracelet that would have stood as a sleeve of an evening dress. This gift is one of my dearest jewels and every time I wear it in my heart there is always a tender and affectionate memory for Amedeo.

La separazione è lo scempio di tutti i desideri fioriti e coltivati accanto a. Ma, sotto sotto, la parte che ha iniziato il suo distacco profondo, sovrintende con soddisfazione alle pratiche del trasloco da. Il trasloco vero e proprio dalla nostra prima e ultima casa, dove avevamo cercato di coniugare le nostre vite, lo attuarono i miei genitori, col pretesto che ero in tournée, mai li ho ringraziati abbastanza per avermi risparmiato quella pena; riuniti i pochi mobili da qualche parte, riparavo a casa loro, nella mia stanza di ragazza a guardare il soffitto e a rimuginare. «È sano lasciarci», mi ripeteva Renato [Mambor], sarà, ma che fatica staccarsi dal quel nostro ‘spazio bianco interamente ricoperto’. Avevo la consapevolezza di avercela messa proprio tutta. Avevo ventisei anni, mi sentivo vecchia, e non prendevo neppure in considerazione che davanti a noi c’era un futuro, anche se non insieme.

Hannah's anorexia [Ceroli] got worse, it was imaginable. When he organized the feast for his eighteen years, both I and Flower [Silver] went. There were a lot of guys, all dressed up. I was fifteen years old and I still remember Anna's lean arms shaking like wicker sticks as she danced fashionable songs of the time. The vision of those thin arms, almost transparent, like the wings of a little bird that wobbles under the sun, still faces my heart. She must have weighed 38 kilos. I felt guilty of her mental illness because I had abandoned her a year earlier, when I had moved to my father, to escape my mother's beatings, her senseless aggression against me and against my slender little body.

At seven or eight years of age I reproached me to become a man. The female world did not seem attractive to me, free and fun as male. Apart from Pia, the only woman I wanted to be. I have never forgiven my father to have pulled away from school only my brother the day that the Soviets announced the success of a man's first mission in space. My brother not me and my sister too! My father, excited and moved, had rushed to our school to announce the historic event to my brother and his companions. Then he brought Roberto home with him, not even being able to imagine spending that day without his son. My sister and I were left in class to finish the daily routine. We were girls. I felt anger and fury. I didn't want to be a female. I wanted to be a male.

Quanta bella e brutta gente negli attici della Roma col garofano. […] La rappresentazione era questa: un divanetto con Craxi, io qualche volta seduta al suo fianco. La gente che gli passava davanti per farsi vedere, ad elemosinare un’incrociata d’occhi. […]Quelle serate […] cominciavano a disgustarmi. Tutti quei cortigiani, la gara a mostrarsi migliore di un altro nella categoria lacchè, il servilismo perfino nel raccattare un mezzo sorriso, erano francamente troppo. Che circo, quello del Gran Bettino…

"Dario, I think the waters broke ..." "The waters?!" Dario exclaims by finally throwing the great newspaper. A taxi is running. Now we are both excited. We hug us. We occupy half a seat. The taxi driver is worried that the child is born there. We arrived in a minute. Salus clinic. They arrange me in a room. Dario is next to me. The obstetrician, the nurse, all very kind, a little for my baby, a little for the interest that always urges the shows, arrives. Then Latania begins: "Pushes Lady pushes". I put all my goodwill ... but nothing happens. Only Dario is on the move, I get a hand decided on the throat, squeezing and pressing. "Dear, I'm already sick ... do you want to be Chozza?" Going back to the years of my very early childhood, when there was war, I am increasingly convinced myself than in the horror and difficulties of that period I encountered two things, two certainties that a child is absolutely needed to live: protection And nourishment. It seems absurd to talk about one and the other when every day, now for now, we lived under the bombs and massacres and was hungry from morning to evening. But there, in Pozzuoli, where I was with my mother and grandparents, every day a ritual happened that managed to satisfy those two needs of certainty. Every evening he expected the last train to pass, and then all the people of the country poured into a long railway tunnel, where the next train would only go to the following dawn. We went inside loads of blankets and any rag that could keep us warm, they formed beds and threw themselves to sleep. A closed and heavy air, and so much frightened humanity. But in those hours under the tunnel we felt protected, and not only because it was believed to be bombs proof, but because we were all there, next to the others, to breathe the same air, to make courage, to even find the game and the joke. It seemed to me that they had purchased a huge family, my protective angels multiplied by ten, percent: safe and protected, with such a precise perception why I would have had in life again. Today I understand that that tunnel, after all, was for me the peace of the unborn child in the womb.

Io non porto rancore a Dario [Fo], perché alla base c’è il mio più grande difetto: quello di non avere ambizione. Non è che Dario abbia mai pensato: «Franca mi dà delle idee e io faccio il furbone!». No, non gli è proprio venuto in mente. Se non fossi stata sua moglie, se fossi stata un’altra, avrebbe dovuto, per forza di cose, mettere anche il mio nome come autrice. Einaudi ha pubblicato numerosi testi di Dario curati da me e inizialmente non figurava neanche la dicitura «a cura di Franca Rame». Quando chiesi a Roberto Cerati la spiegazione di questo, mi rispose: «Non ci avevamo pensato». Il guaio è che sono la moglie, e la moglie, talvolta, è un mobile di casa, un quadro alla parete, qualcosa che ti sta vicino ma che non vedi. Conosco centinaia di sfoghi di donne sull’argomento. Se dovessi rinascere, ma spero proprio che non mi succeda, non cambierei una sola virgola di quanto ho fatto nella mia vita. Con Dario ho vissuto una vita esageratamente felice, l’unica cosa che all’inizio mi disturbava era la sgradevole sensazione di essere trasparente, specialmente con le altre donne. Quante volte arrivavano in Palazzina Liberty le famose femministe, leaderine del movimento, quelle che sanno tutto, che ti parlano dall’alto in basso. Entravano, magari vedevano che stavo pulendo il grande locale con una scopa (ruolo insolito per una prima attrice), non è che se ne accorgessero e dicessero almeno: «Ciao». No, mi chiedevano solo: «Dov'è Dario?». «La peggior nemica della donna... è la donna». Non c'è commedia dove non abbia ribadito il concetto.

Because I know I'm a strong woman, and with a man I want to establish an equality relationship: I don't want to be dominated, but I don't even want to dominate. I had a hard time getting rid of her. And she was not the only one: who knows why I have often aroused these homosexual thoughts and desires. Among my most uninhibited admirers I had a priest, a general and a Japanese journalist. The latter, in particular, was to marry, and on the day of the marriage he did not show up because he had discovered shortly before that the future bride did not give sufficient guarantees as my admirer... The priest? He was texting me post office from New York. And he continued to write to me for years: he addressed me a bit as he would address, in prayers, Our Lady. For Christ's sake, never a hint of sex: he worshiped me as a sacred image.

Con questo libro intendo rispondere alle tante domande che mi vengono sempre poste e alle quali non so mai come replicare. Ma a chi mi chiede «come riesci a tenere separate la tua vita privata da quella professionale?», so cosa dire. Non le separo. Infatti non separo niente di niente. Non mi separo neanche mai da nessuno. Tutto per me si intreccia e si mescola come in un unico minestrone. Non solo non separo la mia vita privata da quella pubblica, ma anche le mie due professioni si mescolano: modella e attrice sono per me lo stesso mestiere. Non riesco a scindere l'infanzia dall’età adulta e, peggio ancora, i vivi dai morti.

\u003cp style="textalign:justify;"\u003eFor years and years I had kept a personal diary, a protected fence to let myself go completely to myself, as if it were a small inner camera in front of which to recite finally my whole part. I started in prison, and I hadn't stopped. In the solitude of writing, I found comfort and companionship and discovered the veins of my unfamiliar voice. I felt safe in my intimacy, as if only there I could really be home. But life, you know, goes down steps, and one spring morning, suddenly, I looked at myself in the mirror and I was afraid. "What will happen to my diary when I'm gone?" I said to myself looking a little further. I am a woman who, albeit emotional and sometimes vulnerable, knows how to make her decisions. I said to Ines and Ninni, and I went into my room. I watched for a long time that black notebook, with a hard cover, which had been a companion of so many thoughts, of so many emotions. I browsed it slightly, without haste. Turning the pages, I smelled the scent of the years, I felt my mood changes from the differences of the handwriting, now harsh, now nervous, now more relaxed. Finally, I went to the bathroom and got a can of matches. One small gesture was enough, and my words became fire, and then ashes. I had no regrets but, at times, a little nostalgia. And I didn't stop writing. From that time, however, every end of the year I entrust myself to a match, as if it were the magical instrument of a small rite between me and myself.

At the age of sixteen I was enrolled among the Little and then Young Italians. We had a uniform with a white shirt, a wide skirt under our knees and a black tie. To go out alone was forbidden... an idea came to me: we took sheets and knotted them and went down. [...] From time to time we made parades with the uniforms in Via dei Fori Imperiali passed in the magazine by the Duce. Once we were at the Mussolini Forum and he passed before us girls. Arriving in front of me, he caressed me. I felt a great emotion: a caress of the Duce. His great charisma had conquered all of us young people. A few years later, during the war, I learned that his favorite films were those of Greta Garbo and mine, and I was very proud of it.

Gli oggetti che decorano il mio appartamento hanno una storia, non sono lì solo per bellezza. C’è la miniatura del mercatino di Napoli, che mi fa pensare a una città che amo tanto ma che vedo di rado. Attorno al mio lavandino, ci sono pietre provenienti ciascuna da un posto per me significativo. Una è di Dannholmen, l’isola svedese dove con mamma passavamo ogni vacanza estiva e dove ha voluto che fossero sparse le sue ceneri. C'è una pietra nera della vulcanica Stromboli, dove i miei genitori si incontrarono, si innamorarono e girarono il film con quel titolo. C’è il sampietrino di Roma, che mi fa pensare alla mia città natale, e al movimento del '68, quando i sampietrini venivano lanciati contro la polizia. [...] Sul comodino vicino al mio letto ci sono Madonne, Cristi, Vishnù, crocifissi, diavoli, anime ardenti, corna e Buddha: tutti insieme, magari uno mi protegge più di un altro, e anche per celebrare la coesistenza delle religioni. C’è il «bee board», il «pannello d’api», che David Lynch ha fatto per me battezzando a una a una le api che aveva trovato morte vicino a una fine stra: Hank, Bob, Dave... ecc, un omaggio al mio amore per gli animali e il suo «chicken kit», kit del pollo, con le istruzioni su come rimettere insieme un pollo macellato, come quelli che si comprano al supermercato.
