Actually, the idea was really annoying to me. I had left behind the insecurity of the past, I no longer needed the experts of the Crazy Horse, the lesson of De Sica I carried inside me. As for the passing of the years, I had learned with experience to make time my best ally: I didn't contrast it, I didn't suffer it, I lived every day for what it was, leaving, like Mona Lisa, my beauty to mature in peace. Marcello and I returned to Altman with the certainty that we were on the right track. He looked at us, he understood and shouted, "Action!" Although fate had separated us in the work, geography, customs, all this by charm vanished in the moment of turning. We were young again for a few hours, ready to bite life, to love it. The striptease of Mara in Yesterday, today, tomorrow, redone a thirty years later on the same languid notes of Abatjour, welcomes with a smile the flow of age and discovers in the vulnerability of each of us the true juice of existence.

Even in the cinema the first effects of the war were felt. I was called back to Rome. He telegraphed the "Celius" that I would return immediately. The return journey was rather exhausting. Although Italy had declared its neutrality, the flame of the great conflict touched it. On trains, in particular, there was evidence of restlessness, discomfort. Travellers seemed concerned: the anxiety of the present, the uncertainty of the future, was seen in their faces. The speeches were all around the war. When I arrived in Rome, I immediately went to the plant, where I was greeted with relief by the staff and the actors, who were waiting for me. Everyone was concerned that the Cines, on which the CelioFilm depended, had placed personal and half-paying actors, on the pretext of war. In our area, these were the first unpleasant consequences of the European conflict. Amazement and irritation for that arbitrary measure can well be guessed. The artists were outraged and everyone was around me hoping that I could fix our situation.

Era l’anno 1927, quando, trovandomi a Parigi, appresi la notizia della morte di Matilde Serao. Il mio dolore fu intenso e profondo. Perdevo un’illustre e grande amica. Il ricordo che io serbavo di lei persisteva nel mio cuore. Io l’ho amata con sentimento di figlia e la rievoco in queste memorie con immutabile devozione. Sento ancora la sua voce, la cui cadenza meridionale era sorretta da un senso esatto e vivo della proprietà di linguaggio. In quest’epoca tumultuosa, turbinante, in cui molte cose di ieri sembrano superate e obliate, le parole di saggezza consolatrice rimangono, non appassiscono.

È solo per dimostrare come anche allora, nell’epoca fortunata del cinema muto, l’Italia fosse all’avanguardia; sia per ardimenti produttivi, sia per larghezza di compensi e di mezzi. Il nostro cinema muto si affermò vittoriosamente su tutti i mercati e fu esclusivamente italiano senza coproduzioni straniere, senza l’aiuto di “vedettes” internazionali: non ce n’era bisogno! Fu, lo ripeto, il primo del mondo, e sarebbe bene, oggi, non dimenticarlo!

Claudia Cardinale was different from the actresses I had known, a little by nature, a little for the hard experiences lived. Araba (she loves to define herself like that), her innate introversion was accentuated by her life marked early: she had to pretend to be her son's sister for many years. He suffered hypocrisy, the violence of certain people full of prejudice and contempt for women, even worse for actresses. She curled herself, accumulating anger that only she could turn into smiles. [...] She was watched, perpetually on a diet; they counted every bite of her. His court was annoyed by my intrusion; they didn't understand what I wanted. The rule was that everyone was with their clan and that if I didn't have mine, it was worse for me. I pretended nothing; I deliberately wanted to break certain balances, stupid habits; I meant to displace them with my way of doing it. They were very annoyed because they couldn't say anything; after all I counted as much as Claudia in the movie and if I wanted to talk to her I could knock and chat all I wanted. I was intrusive, bullying; I had fun. One day I said to her: "Come to me, I have to show you something."I had nothing to show her, but in doing so I could take her away from surveillance and offer her a little truce. From that moment I pulled it out of the group. At lunchtime I went to find her with an excuse and in the meantime I offered her some chocolate; we laughed. I started to tell her a little bit about my life, my thoughts. I was talking to her about the things I wanted to do, how I was doing, some disappointments. She listened silently. This is how our complicity was born, slowly.

Quando i miei figli saranno grandi venderò la casa e andrò a vivere sull’isola, perché non mi trovo a mio agio nel mondo, ma quando sono lì provo un senso enorme di pace, quando cammino per i suoi viottoli sterrati, quando nuoto nelle sue acque, quando respiro la macchia mediterranea che profuma di mirto e rosmarino, sto bene, sento la vita scorrere nelle vene. La conosco meglio di me stessa e la amo tanto quanto alcune persone asseriscono di amare Dio. Quest’isola è magnetica, infatti, la sua terra è rossa come il ferro e ti attrae e ti avviluppa e se la lasci fare ti entrerà dentro per non lasciarti mai. Questa isola è il mio minuscolo paradiso. Qui e qui solo (è sull’isola che sto scrivendo) mi sento felice e protetta. Qui la gente mi conosce da quando ero bambina, prima di avere successo e tutte le cazzate che ne sono conseguite, qui nessuno mi giudica, qui esce fuori la mia vera essenza.

Lavoravo in Rai, in un programma di grande prestigio. […]. Io, fra gli autori, ero certamente la più sfrenata in quanto a fantasia, capace sempre di inventare un nuovo modo per raccontare le solite cose, con un profondo rispetto per la verità […] e curiosamente puntigliosa nei dettagli. […] Il comitato di redazione decise che [si sarebbe dovuto] intervistare Cesare Romiti, allora consigliere delegato della Fiat […] fui incaricata di contattarlo e di convincerlo a rilasciare l’intervista. […].

Naturalmente, anche Franco era coinvolto in queste mie scelte. Sebbene non venisse mai sul set, evitando di esporsi ufficialmente, lavorava prima sul copione, quando c'era la lettura del personaggio, e in base a quello, una volta stabiliti i termini e firmato il contratto, eliminava le scene di nudo. Se la produzione accettava, stava tranquillo, perché io ero tutelata dalla mia firma e da quella della produzione.

Le tre complici hanno protetto la mia menzogna. «Tutto bene, promossa». Quell'anno d'indipendenza è fra i miei ricordi più singolari. Mi svegliavo felice in quella casa estranea con il piacevole senso di estraneità che mi danno gli alberghi, prendevo il caffè con l'impagabile cugina che mi parlava del mio lavoro come se esistesse. Avevo ormai molti amici e tante telefonate, ero decisamente corteggiata.

E anche lì, visto che la maggior parte me li sono scritti io, dovrei peccare di modestia oppure di presunzione, considerando che hanno avuto tutti un buon esito perché prima di farli ci ho pensato so io quanto.

Io intanto vivevo la mia vita come potevo, nascosta dietro un velo sottile ma tenace di timidezza. Sì, è diffìcile da credere, ma ero veramente timida, forse anche per via della nostra condizione. Mio padre non c’era e mia madre era troppo bionda, alta, viva, e soprattutto non sposata. La sua bellezza eccentrica e sopra le righe mi metteva in imbarazzo. Sognavo una mamma normale e rassicurante, con i capelli neri, il grembiule stropicciato, le mani rovinate e gli occhi stanchi.
Ero avvelenata dalla rabbia e di fronte alle sue parole di scuse, reagii dandole una solenne sberla. Questo era ed è il mio “caratteraccio” toppo impulsivo. Ero proprio una discolaccia!

Oggi, rispetto ai miei tempi, le donne probabilmente sono più fortunate, vengono giudicate sulla base di quello che sanno fare più che sul loro aspetto. Ma, aumentando la possibilità di scegliere, aumentano anche le difficoltà nel bilanciare famiglia e lavoro. Il mondo è più complesso, più esigente, e richiede alle ragazze grandi sacrifìci, proporzionati alle soddisfazioni. Eppure, alla fine, si torna sempre allo stesso punto e ognuna di noi fa i conti con se stessa. [...] L’ho detto tante volte, e lo ripeto anche oggi: sono loro, i miei figli, i miei film migliori. Ed è la loro felicità il premio che mi onora di più.

La verità mi incuriosisce più delle bugie. […] È che a me piace ridere, anche lì, farlo come un gioco, perché è diverso da tutto il resto della vita. È uno spazio di libertà. Domani sera ho la prima, non posso pensare ad altro. Che fortuna, tra poco avrò un'altra storia. Io e quello che mi porto dietro, per due ore non esisteremo più.

I act coldly: the gestures are safe, I choose the most succulent things, where there are fats, sugars, everything that can be harmful, poisonous, that definitely hurts. That makes you fat with shame. [...] With food women betray, rebel, poison, forget. Now I'm going to go to bed, sugar will make me sleep like a baby after he sucked Mom's milk.

Fellini amava il personaggio di Carla […] che rappresentava il sogno erotico, l’amante per antonomasia, così morbida, remissiva, compiacente, quasi stupida, scevra di ogni irritante rivendicazione femminile. […] era l’amante perfetta a titolare le fantasie erotiche degli uomini, mentre io non andavo pazza per lei. […] L’idea tutta maschile che Fellini aveva di un prototipo femminile fatto di piume e violette non mi assomigliava per niente. Ora mi è chiaro per quale motivo la odiassi. Non posso pensare a Carla senza associarla alla morte di mia madre. Però il ruolo dell’amante fu in un certo senso profetico perché traslò dallo schermo alla realtà.

Alone, helped by my father's watchful and constant love, I took the reins of the first Company Marta Abba in 1929, all of young people led and directed by me.  [...] I followed to carry around my major interpretations of the Pirandelli repertoire. [...] wise men and experts of the theatre [...] catalogued my repertoire as a brain and only artistic (i.e. anti-commercial). And the voice spread to the public. Cerebral Marta Abba is great only in piradellian art. [...] And so the year after a second Company of my formation, with a repertoire that was increasingly increasing of new interpretations, among which I like to remember above all that of Madame Legros, Penelope of Maugham, The Good Fairy of Molnar, Our companion of Antoine, and Anna Kapina taken from the novel of Tolstoi and the Cricket of the hearth taken from the novel of Dickens.

In the movies I have been all kinds of women: the people, the bourgeois, the woman object, also the woman in career. And in all of them there was also something of what I myself am: something that belonged to me privately, deeply, even secretly: the fact is (I have already explained...) that I did not base everything on the technique of acting. On the contrary, to be able to recite it, I need to live it, my character. The final result is that I left shreds of Claude and Claudia in all my movies. I am the Angelica del Gattopardo: I belong to his strength, his realism, the stubborn will to go on, the stubbornness. But I am also the protagonist of the film by Squitieri Act of Pain: the mother who ends up killing her son who is the victim of drugs, in a crescendo of tragedy that belongs to me, also that, and that I know very well. And I am the Ida of Comencini's History, with its absolute, total passivity: a creature on which they pass, trampling it, life and history, without her being able to do anything to oppose and defend herself. Because, yes, I am strong, stubborn, volitional, but I can also be very passive. It is a passivity, a fatalism, that come from far away: Africa, Tunis... And I am also Aida, of the Girl with the suitcase: it is mine, authentic, her sadness. I am Carla of the Indifferent: I can also be indifferent or, perhaps, more than indifferent, fatalist. I am the devil, the perdition, the female that destroys, as I have always seen that genius of Bolognini in all the films that I made with him: The beautiful Anthony, The Viaccia, Senility. It is that part of me that I find especially in my adolescence and early youth: when I used my strength, my physicality, my power of seduction for revenge and, by ransom, compared to the horror of which I had been the victim... I think in particular of the protagonist of Viaccia: an extremely cruel woman. I'm not. I've never really been cruel. But I was a woman full of grudges: so I put in the character of the film my grudge full of sadness.

[In tournée con ] Carlo Campanini, Franco Scandurra […] e Sandra Mondaini […] arrivati a Foggia io volli andare a San Giovanni Rotondo per vedere un frate di cui si parlava molto, Padre Pio. […] Campanini, figlio spirituale del padre, ci accompagnò da questo straordinario uomo. […] Anche io diventai sua figlia spirituale e con Campanini facemmo metà di un guanto che Padre Pio usava per coprire le stigmate. Campanini ed io avevamo l’abitudine di recitare ogni giorno il rosario.

After the separation of my parents grew in me the sense that I had been abandoned by my parents, but from this abandonment also gave rise to my great freedom. I began to take advantage of it, to combine every one. As an misunderstood and mortified child, I became independent. I started smoking the first cigarettes in elementary school, once I even made a joint with the nanny, I was nine years old. In the evening I threw myself on the couch and devoured old movies, smoking cigarettes and drinking a glass of water, pretending it was liqueur. I imitate what I saw, I also wanted to be free as my parents, to throw everything at me. After my father had left my mother's home, there was nothing left but a shadow that taught rules, often random, that no one cared to respect. Rules she forgot the next day. The most absurd was just to go around the building with my white bike, because going around the neighborhood was dangerous. But maybe she didn't imagine that it was that danger that attracted me. Anyway, it would have been worse to stay home.

My luck began with the meeting [...] [with] Sergio Amidei. And Amidei replied with his sarcasm: "It's called Lilia Silvi and it is said that it will take your place". The reaction of the actress was instinctive: he got up from the table and threw bread crumbs in my direction.

Finché lo scandalo non serpeggiò come un brivido nella sala e sembrò che persino l’orchestra esitasse. Ebbi la percezione d’essere la sola persona felice in quel  lungo momento di disagio generale. Avevo colpito nel segno.

Too bad that she was a villain, Marcello and I met for the first time in a beautiful black and white and fell in love with each other immediately. Cinematographically, of course. I remember the first time I saw him again. I was on the stairs of the palace where we would turn, and he, from a few floors up, would look at me. Hello, he greeted me as if it were in the air. Hello, I replied, shy and excited.Ours was an immediate harmony, which remained intact until the end, without ever a crack. In that first set we met in the background of the happy and luminous Rome of the post-war period, where people had resumed to travel, to go to the sea, to take a bath, to love and to have fun. We were good together, Marcello and I. What a couple! Simple, beautiful, real.

Carlo Levi had said at the time of the allied victory: "And now what do we do? [...] The mental habit of living according to the stimulus of such a great opponent to fight which had been necessary every resource of energy, intellectual, ideal, had made the antifascists armed only for supreme struggles. The position of the Italian anti-fascist was a generous, heroic, idealistic position, far from any concrete political program. Especially for young people, it was difficult. They had never posed the problem of having to happen suddenly to the legacy of a government, irreparably spoiled in its bureaucratic structure, at a time when the presence of the allies, the testing of democracy made it very risky. They had no memory of previous democratic governments. [...] They had fought against fascism since adolescence, isolated, looked at you with suspicion and some rebuke from their families. In some of them the anti-fascist rebellion had arisen late, already under military shoes, among the atrocities of that megaloman war. They had gone to fight after September 8 in partisan formations, controversially forgetting their military degrees, their cultural premises, living only in the ideality of that common struggle. Instead, they found the old strong and serene as if nothing had happened. [...] Elections passed: the Christian Democrats won.  [...] The slim flame of the MSI sprang up. In Parliament, they sat down overflowing men who had killed, persecuted, praised, the most horrible events that had ever offended the civilized world. They sat beside the exiles, the anti-fascists, as in their homes. Some of them had spent, as atonement, a few months in prison or in the countryside and boasted of it as a martyr. Next to them sat the quailists, the macabre jesters of our tragedy. Young people weren't there. [...] Italy found itself so, after its long fearful adventure, still in the hands of politicians. His democratic expression, after so much blood, bore the MSI, the Qualunquism. Even democracy, so badly conquered, mocked martyrs. The peers disappeared from the scene.

Before they got divorced, they had a lot of fight, and they had a lot of fight about me, too. I was the most interesting in the family in terms of foster care: I was underage and I worked a lot. Success had come suddenly, dragging me and, by the time, I made a lot of money. There have been raging struggles between them. I was entrusted to my mother, but it was my father who signed my contracts and held the money. Shortly after custody my mother said to me: "If you want to be with me in Paris, you have to help me economically. "At the time she had no financial concern. My father had left her half the apartment we had lived in, and gave her a more than correct monthly. He lived well. I was only seventeen years old and often, I think, I would have the right not to be forced to work. I could have studied; I could have chosen another job. I needed to be protected, not loved. When she said, "If you want to stay, you absolutely have to provide economically," I left. I came to live in Italy. I think the hardest thing I've learned in my life has been to accept that I'm not loved.

One of the living testimonies of the unhappiness of the actresses, linked to the fear of aging, was Rita Hayworth for me. We shot the circus and his great adventure. At forty -five years old, what had been the "atomic", one of the most beautiful women in American and world cinema, was completely breakdown. But he drank and, drinking, forgot: he seemed not to make himself absolutely account of the physical state in which he had reduced himself. He was drinking, even in front of me. Then he looked at me, and he said to me: "But I'm still beautiful ..." Straziante. It will also be for this reason that I have never only focused on my body. Yes, for the press I was a sex symbol; But in the cinema, fortunately, no; I was above all the actress of the great masters. It was not all and only my merit: the Africa from which I come has taught me to deeply respect fate, luck ...

Thank God I never got confused with the girl who appeared photographed in the papers. Otherwise, I would have lost my mind. People are sometimes surprised that my friend Alain Delon talks about himself in the third person. The fact is that you usually ignore what a star's life means; rumors about us, clouds of courtiers, servants who make us see the world as a vast satellite. The more we are idolized, the stronger we must be in order not to lose our identity as human beings. I was lucky to have always been aware of my identity. When I look on the screen, I know it's not really me, but someone who looks like me. I've never confused roles. And when I meet someone on the street who smiles at me, I'm just happy, as if he compliments me for working well. I've never tried to seduce, to lie, I've just tried to make people dream.

Yes: so, how do you seduce, or seduced, Claudia Cardinale? You have to be equipped with authentic charm. You have to have a look worthy of the name. And then I like the mystery: I like a person to discover; While I don't like people totally clear and open, perhaps because they have never been easy to get easy. Here then explained that I am of those who fall in love first with the head, and only after with the body. From a man, I want it to be strong. Because I know I am a strong woman, and with a man I want to establish a relationship of equality: I don't want to be dominated, but I don't even want to dominate. It will be for this reason that, even getting older, I have never happened to make a fantasy on a much younger man: I am enjoying me that the boys, when I pass on the street, tell me their admiration, but everything ends there. Nor have I ever made a sentimental or erotic fantasy on a woman. While I happened to have been the subject of this type of attention from some women: there was an Arab princess who chased me for years, she sent me even embarrassing gifts, very expensive jewels, for example, and wrote me .. I was very difficult to get rid of her. And it was not the only one: who knows why I often aroused these homosexual thoughts and desires. Among my most uninhibited admirers I had a Japanese priest, general and journalist. The latter, in particular, had to get married, and the wedding day did not show up because he had discovered just before the future bride did not give sufficient guarantees like my admirer ... the priest? He wrote me stationary from New York. And he continued to write to me for years: he turned to me a bit like he would turn to the prayers to the Madonna. For heaven's sake, never a hint of sex: it venerated me like a sacred image.

But if they left, those objects certainly had a reason. I never had constancy in looking for them, after a small look back, stealthy, polite not to offend them, I continued. Every now and then I think of them, their temperature, their color, and I'm sure they are fine where they have chosen to be. But I also have loyal objects, stubborn, of which I would like to undo, but they remain stuck my whole life for no reason. And then I end up loving them. The amount of those I lose, compared to those who remain, is very great. [...] These are the things that move away from me. [...] I fall in love with certain things. They understand it, they don't want to be a prisoner and leave. [...] I need to touch them and maybe they can't stand to be loved. I feel they have a slight rebellion towards me. A need for freedom. They get stolen from the first that happens, hide in mysterious places. [...] They told me that it is I who let them fall [...] Maybe I don't have a good grip, that is, I have no strength in my hands. I have never been possessive. I prefer that objects choose me, rather than choosing them.

Ebbi due soli amici d’infanzia: un coniglio grigio che si chiamava Bambo e una gallina che chiamai Benita come quel Mussolini ch’era già sulla bocca di tutti. Con loro, soprattutto con Benita, mi trovavo bene e cominciai ad accorgermi che la mia bontà di cuore era solo per gli animali: le persone mi erano indifferenti o antipatiche, le loro gioie e dolori non mi interessavano affatto.

Il cinema era muto: effetti e parole glieli davamo noi, gli attori, con arte nuova e autentica. Esigevo perciò che nessuno toccasse i miei negativi. Soltanto io dovevo montarli. Tagliare i positivi è una cosa, ma tagliare i negativi è un’altra. Io, allora, tagliavo i negativi. E non potevo permettere che si facesse qualcosa senza di me. Fingevo persino d’ignorare che esistessero gli stabilimenti di sviluppo e montaggio.

The next day they enrolled me from the nuns, to the elementary school in Piazza Vittorio, an ancient building, with a large garden full of trees where, as soon as I could and nobody saw me, I took off my shoes and walked barefoot on the grass because I wanted to hear the Sassolini and the coolness under my feet, like when I lived in the countryside. It was an operation that I was secretly made because the nuns would not have approved it since they wanted to make me a Signorinella educating me according to the best etiquette, just me who came from the countryside and I spoke Dialètti and an idiot's cretin did not go to do the spy?! "Put this on," said the technician, tending me to a swimsuit, with such sudden ways that they left me breathless. And to think I was there with the producer. "I wonder how they would treat me if I was here alone," I asked myself with horror. I changed behind the screen and went back in front of them. I felt naked, and my shyness burned like a wound. Indifferent, the cameramen lengthened a cigarette, ordering me to light it and then to walk back and forth, looking at the target. I had never smoked before, nor had I ever been alone in front of a camera. I felt that I was completely inadequate, and the operator seemed to agree with me. "Dotto" is impossible to photograph. It has too short a face, too wide a mouth, too long a nose!It's like always, I looked too much again. And yet I was like this, what was my fault?

Camminavamo in silenzio. Pure avrei voluto dire tante cose: manifestargli i miei sentimenti, l’ammirazione che avevo per lui; ma tacevo. Tuttavia un sentimento d’orgoglio sopravvenne in me. Egli non era un uomo come tutti gli altri. Era uno scienziato, un genio, colui che aveva dischiuso al mondo contemporaneo e futuro le più ardite e benefiche meraviglie della scienza, collegando attraverso le onde invisibili dell’etere tutti i continenti, tutti i popoli, tutte le anime umane. Egli era lì con me e mi aveva chiesto di unire il mio destino al suo. Ma io cosa potevo rispondere? L’affetto che avevo per lui non si era ancora mutato in una dedizione d’amore. Occorreva perciò attendere che il mio sentimento per lui si trasformasse in qualcos’altro di ben diverso, di più serio. Ma io non cercavo un marito.

The first to believe in me for sin that it is a rogue was Suso Cecchi D’Amico, the only woman in the parterre of the great screenwriters of the time. "The oak that made a lot of Italian cinema flourish among its branches" as Lina Wertmüller defines it. Reading the Roman tales of Moravia, Suso had come across a beautiful story that was right at the beginning of the new fanatic. I remember that we met one day on the train. After having glimpsed me at the station, he came to my compartment and sat down next to me, with a confidence that immediately put me at ease. "I have a subject for you in mind," he said simply, looking at me straight in the eyes. "Why not?!" I replied immediately with my usual momentum. Suso had worked on the story with Flaiano, Moravia and Persenza, and had proposed it to Blasetti, who at the time was looking for the subject for a film. [...] After our short railway meeting Suso, supported by Flaiano and Blasetti himself, insisted with the producers until the part is obtained for me.

Sono anni ormai che condivido la mia vita con questi pensieri. All’inizio mi dissero che era depressione, e provai a curarla con le medicine. Ancora oggi assumo quattro diverse pillole prima di andare a dormire. Dormire, in realtà, non è la parola corretta, sarebbe meglio dire cercare di dormire. Soffro di insonnia da quando ero una bambina. Mi ricordo che una volta mentre ero sotto l’effetto di alcuni psicofarmaci tremendi che mi erano stati prescritti, un giornalista mi disse: «Che sguardo profondo che hai, sembra che mi stai guardando nell’anima». E invece ero solo anestetizzata, pensa te. Ma queste cure con me non hanno mai funzionato, perché se da un lato non sentire niente mi calmava, dall’altro quell’apatia totale mi snaturava. Una come me che va in giro senza pelle, con i nervi, i muscoli e i tendini scoperti, preda delle intemperie, preda di tutto, prima di tutto di me stessa, ferita sanguinante, come può non sentire più niente? Vestire una pelle morta? Impossibile. E dove ho provato a metterci una pezza con la droga, non ho fatto che allargare la crepa.

Mi capita anche di pensare, prima di dormire, all'efficienza fisica di cui dispongo. Non è tutta fortuna, mi dico, l'ho aiutata soprattutto nel non farmene un problema. Penso che il corpo ci sia grato di lasciargli godere la sua libertà di esprimersi fornendogli senza impegno quegli aiuti non chimici che la nostra amicizia gli deve fornire. Non sono mai stata avara di anticorpi, ogni volta che ho raccolto un boccone o un cucchiaino caduti a terra e me li sono messi in bocca ho sentito la sua gratitudine. E mi ha sorriso benevolo quando si entrava in un locale e mentre le amiche si precipitavano a lavarsi le mani io mi sedevo tranquillamente a tavola, tutt'al più sfilandomi i guanti, che porto perché soffro il freddo e mi piacciono molto.

We went down to Ventotene, I, with the strength of Michelangelo's gaze [Antonions], entered the island. [...] The slight noises, of which I did not know the origin, the different planes and the lights that filtered put me a great euphoria. [...] I went forward on the branches and the leaves, then I turned and I was alone. Alone. [...] I could no longer hear the sea and the voices. I waited a few minutes, then I tried to go back. It was hard. I didn't know where I was. I called, no answer. I decided to go straight in front of me, without ever turning around, I would have had to get to the sea. I did it. I saw the boat. Senza aver bisogno di alcuna confidenza, Visconti aveva capito tutto della violenza che nascondevo. To see me smiling again, Dad Giuseppe built me ​​a small theater, I remember him very well: an axis and a backdrop. It was simple, without puppets, but I created them in my imagination, I pulled the invisible threads and the stories came to life by magic. That theater made me dream, it was my fantastic place. That my future was elsewhere, I felt it. I felt that one day not far away the hour of detachment would come and I feared it. My brothers and playmates warned that I was not like them and sometimes they made me understand, making fun of me. The tender memory of that first small theater of mine always accompanies me and I like to think of it as an omen of the fate that awaited me. Later, much later over the years, I had another small theater: the immense small theater in via Rovello. And this time I was the puppet, a puppet aware that being linked to the wires of Giorgio Strehler was an unparalleled discovery of new horizons: in the effort of work, in the freedom of feelings, in the daily life of life. A life that tells her it seems the plot of a novel.

I was considered an intellectual actress. For Pinter's Old Times, Luchino told me with his Milanese accent: "You, on stage you will have to be naked! You can misread the text, you can recite it in an aberrant way. No one pays attention to what you say. When I realized that power over the audience I found the wonderful thing! Luchino had emptied the audience and put a ring in the center of the Argentina theatre in Rome. I was there, in the middle, completely naked and much of the city had come to see me "it was always in the twentieth century " and at that moment I realized that I no longer had any problem with acting. It was all sorted out.

My daughter has the same problems with me as I have with my parents. My photos appeared in the massive Lacôme advertising campaign around the world for fourteen years in a row. When Elettra was born, my image was already in magazines, TV, posters and billboards. One day, at kindergarten, the teacher taught children what to do in case they were lost: to look for a policeman, to memorize their address and telephone number, and other solutions like that. Then he began to question the children. He asked my daughter, "What would you do if you got lost at an airport?" and she: "I'd look for the billboard with my mom and wait for help under there." In fact, Elettra thought that all people in advertising were mothers and fathers who were exposed everywhere, in airports, roads and highways, in the event that their children would get lost and need help.

Io recito solo se davanti a me c’è una macchina da presa: non mi interessa da sola, così all’improvviso, per esempio far finta di essere un albero che perde le foglie. Fabio [Mauri] and I were both disturbed, though in a different way: we looked alike. Instead, I couldn't feel comfortable with her parents. They had a very conventional mindset. Eventually, the marriage turned out to be a scary mistake. Fabio, however, had no fault of it except that he became, in spite of him, a close relative of mine. [...] The marriage had lasted two years, and it took many more to separate me definitively. Then there was no divorce, so it was necessary to ask for the annulment of the Sacred Rota. After an infinite time, I was summoned to the tribunal of the Holy See for questioning. [...] Thus began the cancellation process which at the time lasted years, years and years. From time to time, friends and relatives and even Professor Musatti were called to testify: an infinite procedure. When the referendum on divorce arrived in 1970, the tribunal of the Holy See was still studying my case, examining the papers, grinding papyri, evaluating the testimonies. In my life I never participated in a piazza demonstration, but when they won the no, that same night I went dancing wearing a black dress by Dior with a velvet collar where the inscription "NO" was in rhinestone. The divorce law had finally passed and, suddenly, waiting for the verdict of the Sacred Rota was no longer necessary.

Un luogo immaginario, illusorio. Il mio psicoanalista era, anche lui, molto particolare. Alla fine ha scritto per me Tre uomini per Amalia, dramma che purtroppo ho recitato soltanto dopo la sua morte, Non era un’opera eccezionale ma diciamo che vi serpeggiava un fondo di verità. Poi anch’io ho scritto per lui Caro Professore, una commedia che ha avuto anche un certo successo.

With the bed [Anna Magnani] the orgy of freedom became frenzy, in Rome. Ingenious magazines were composed for her, who animated her with a pathos and a disturbing irony. He erected in Rome, from the torture of one of the most unhappy and intelligent peoples, an explosion of truths that would have dispersed forever, and not only on the field of scenic expression, the last misunderstandings of an empty aestheticism. [...] It was the bed, from those naked stages, to send that message. From a film shot with her clandestinely, when the nightmare of the German occupation still chuckled the blood, that great face of consciousness and pain, came, crossing the sea and the boundaries, all over the world; And people from all over the world, disturbed, welcomed the story of Pietà: to the bed, to De Sica, Rossellini, Visconti, Zavattini, we owe a great spiritual moment of our time, perhaps it is necessary if the Our mortified suffering, our misery, had interpreters capable of making them understand and love after so much insulation. Bruna and powerful sat in the midst of everyone looking around with phosphorescent flashes of the gray eyes, in a paradoxical attitude of rest, the semiaperta mouth in a half generous and bitter smile. [...] For someone's solicitation he got up like a cat removed from his torpor and with a thoughtful slowness, almost solemn, he walked towards the scene; The subtle life, erected on the vast kidneys, the live hair, twisted in disturbing disorder. At his entry, from the audience, through the scenes, something that was not noise came, but like the anxious of a tumulting held back by an irresistible silence. Only the voice, the thousand voices of the bed, burlesque, childish, brown of sudden sadness, were imposed on that silence by loading it more and more emotion. The wide gesture of the arms raised to disperts the sad and superb head, seemed to conclude by freeing it, all the intensity that his words and his song had evoked so far. The public exploded, then, with enthralling impetus. He asked for countless encore, especially of the Roman Stornelli where for the first time he heard criticizing, irrigating them, all those things that had tortured him until then. And the deliberately nasal voice of the bed, from Monellaccio Romano, insinuated himself, stimulating the Pasquinata, to the motteggio, to the rebellion. Then, in a moment, his flickering face in a grotesque lazzo relaxed in the vastness of melancholy; His voice resurrected from the dark depth of the chest, and all those people passed from rice to cry with the same prodigious mutuality of the artist. [...] Massimo Bontempelli I led us once. He too had a profound impression. - How does it maintain that lucidity of diction, that supervised scenic intelligence in time to abandon? - He wondered.

Claudia Cardinale was different from the actresses I had known, a little by nature, a little for the hard experiences lived. Araba (she loves to call herself like this), her innate introversion was accentuated by life marked early: she had to pretend her son's sister for many years. He suffered hypocrisy, the violence of certain people full of prejudices and contempt for women, worse for the actresses. He curled up in herself, accumulating anger that only she managed to turn into smiles. [...] was supervised, perpetually on a diet; They counted every bite. His court was dried by this intrusion of mine; They didn't understand what I wanted. The rule was that everyone was with his clan and that if I didn't have mine, worse for me. I pretended nothing; I deliberately wanted to break certain balances, stupid habits; I intended to displace them with my way of doing. They were very annoyed because they couldn't say anything; After all I counted how much Claudia in the film and if I wanted to talk to her I could knock and put myself to chat how much I wanted. I was intrusive, overbearing; I enjoy it. One day I said to her: "Come to me, I have to show you something." I had nothing to show them, but in doing so I could subtract it from surveillance and offer them some respite. From that moment I pulled it out of the group. At the time of lunch I went to look for her with an excuse and in the meantime I also offered some chocolate; We laughed. I began to tell you a little about my life, about my thoughts. After the separation of mine, the sense of having been abandoned by my parents grew in me, but from this abandonment my great freedom also arose. I began to take advantage of it, to combine with each. As a misunderstood and mortified child I became independent. I started smoking the first cigarettes in elementary school, once I also made a rod with the nanny, I was nine years old. In the evening I threw myself on the sofa and devoured old films, smoking cigarettes and drinking a glass of water, pretending that it was liqueur. I imitated what I saw, I also wanted to be free like my parents, slamming everything. After my father had gone from home, my mother was only a shadow that learns rules, often random, that nobody cared for respect. Rules that she herself forgot the next day. The most absurd was to do only the tour of the building with my white bike, because going around the neighborhood was dangerous. But maybe she didn't imagine that it was just that danger that attracted me. In any case, it would have been worse to stay at home.

During the summer season, and strictly after finishing our homework, we were allowed to go to the zoo of the public gardens. One of my playmates came from Trieste and, after his father's death, had come to live in Milan with his mother, a cellist, at 55 Corso Buenos Aires, very close to my home. His name was George. Giorgio Strehler. Many years later and in another situation he looked me in the eye and said, "But then you are the little girl who spoke only dialect." "Yes," I smiled. "The only child among three boys. I was already in love with you." This memory deeply moved us. It is one of the many mysterious threads that have guided me in life, as if the case were amused to make me fall into those spaces that would then become the places around which I would have lived my destiny in the future. When the war broke out, I was six years old when it ended 11. My eyes were full of images I couldn't erase anymore. If I think of the first memories, I feel the bombs, the sound of the anti-aircraft siren, the bites of hunger. And then the cold, the darkest dark. Every once in a while, suddenly, I get scared again. It sounds incredible, but I still sleep with the light on today. First came the Germans, who were our allies at first. In the morning they marched under the house, tall, blond, with blue eyes, and I watched them from the window, kidnapped, divided between fear and excitement. At my look as a child they did not seem bad or dangerous, but the grandparents, who sometimes happened to listen without will, spoke of Jews and deportations, tortures and torn nails, reprisals and betrayals, and gave a very different image. At that point, I ran into the kitchen and questioned them, but they denied it. "No avimm said nothing" answered impassible. The truth is that we were in the eye of the cyclone and we would have noticed it soon. Slowly everything stopped the school, the cinema theater Sacchini, the concerts of the band in the square, everything except the bombs.

In the sixties I shot with Vadim La Ronde, played by many famous actors; among many also Jane Fonda. It was during that film that Vadim left Catherine [Deneuve] for Jane. The objects that decorate my apartment have a history, they're not just there for beauty. There is the miniature of the market of Naples, which makes me think of a city that I love so much but that I rarely see. Around my sink, there are stones each from a significant place for me. One is Dannholmen, the Swedish island where we spent every summer holiday with Mother and where she wanted her ashes to be scattered. There is a black stone of Stromboli volcanic, where my parents met, fell in love and shot the movie with that title. There is the sampietrino of Rome, which reminds me of my hometown, and the movement of 1968, when the sampietrini were launched against the police. [...] On the bedside table next to my bed there are Madonnas, Christs, Vishnus, crucifixes, devils, fiery souls, horns and Buddha: all together, perhaps one protects me more than another, and also to celebrate the coexistence of religions. There is the "bee board", the "pannello d'api", which David Lynch made for me baptizing to one by one the bees that had found death near a strange end: Hank, Bob, Dave... etc., a tribute to my love for animals and his "chicken kit", chicken kit, with instructions on how to put together a butchered chicken, like those that you buy at the supermarket.

Tornando a casa, volli restare sola. Andai in biblioteca. Avevo ansia di rivedere il mio Picasso, l’Arlecchino bleu, che avevo posto nello scaffale di centro che sporgeva dalla libreria. Il dipinto di Picasso scintillava nella austerità di quella sala dalle pareti di damasco rosso antico. Dall’altra parte della biblioteca c’erano altri quadri famosi: un delizioso Bucher, un Fragonard. Dall’antitesi tra questa pittura pervasa di spirito settecentesco e l’ardire dell’opera geniale di Picasso, destinata ad improntare di sé il tempo moderno, nasceva un insieme di colori, di toni che, pur contrastando tra loro, creavano forti armonie. In quella biblioteca amavo raccogliermi fra i ritratti dei miei migliori amici. Forse anch’essi amanti della solitudine come lo ero io. [...] Le donne in genere vanno all'Opera per ostentare il lusso dei loro abiti, dei loro gioielli. In quanto a me, la cosa era ben diversa. Io amavo la musica, sollievo alla mia giornata di snervante lavoro. Di solito arrivavo in teatro tardi, a spettacolo già iniziato, e ne uscivo sempre prima della fine. Detestavo qualsiasi forma di esibizione. Il mistero più completo avvolgeva la mia vita.

On the contrary, to be able to recite it, I need to live it, my character. The final result is that I left Tangelli by Claude, and Claudia, in all my films. I am the Angelica of the Gattopardo: I belong to me his strength, his realism, the stubborn will to go on, the stubbornness. But I am also the protagonist of Squitieri's film Act of pain: the mother who ends up killing the victim of drugs, in a crescendo of tragedy that belongs to me, also that, and that I know very well. And I am the Ida of the history of Comencini, with its absolute, total, passivity: a creature on which they pass, trampling it, life and "history", without being able to do anything to counter and defend herself. Because, yes, I am strong, stubborn, strong -willed, but I can also be very passive. It is a passivity, a fatalism, that come from far away: Africa, Tunis ... and I am also Aida, of the girl with the suitcase: it is mine, authentic, her sadness. I am Carla of the indifferent: I can also be indifferent or, perhaps, more than indifferent, fatalistic. I am the devil, the perdition, the female that destroys, as I have always seen that genius of Bolognini in all the films I have made with him: the beautiful antonio, the way, senility. It is that part of me that I find above all in my adolescence and early youth: when I used my strength, my physicality, my power of seduction for revenge and, for redemption, compared to the horror that I had been a victim ... I think In particular to the protagonist of the Viaccia: an extremely cruel woman. I am not: I've never been really cruel. But I was a woman full of grudge: so I put my grudge full of sadness into the character of the film.

They explained to me that they were always traveling and listed their repertoire: Shakespeare, Platuto, Goldoni, Giacosa... I was very attracted to the idea, but also amazed. I was wondering, who knows why they ask me, who have never acted in my life? However, I decided to follow them. And not because I had the sacred fire of art. No, I didn't even know what that was. I followed them because they were leaving.

Nanna hated bourgeois hypocrisy. He had confusing ideas about these things, but grandiose of rebellion and courage. His disgusting speech, his taste for people with resounding vices, was born from a deep hatred of bourgeois conventions and from the pleasure he felt in reviling them. She had known life early, as a child, and at the age of fourteen, beautiful and weird as a foal, she was found to have to obey the hierarchical laws of the stage in one of the most elegant drama companies that delighted Italy. Those famous, exquisite actors understood absolutely nothing of the personality of that young girl with unpredictable, revolting, drizzling, stubborn like a gypsy. They continued to give her obvious parts in bourgeois comedies where they discolored that sculptural diction, as a plautine mime, which flowed from the sound throat, and which she had learned, barefoot, in the streets of Rome, when in the summer they become a triumph of semi-naked children. She was one of them, the Nanna, and all her blood boiled with that malice and that melancholy.

\ u003cp style = "Textalign: justify;" \ u003eper years and years I had held a personal diary, a protected fence where to let myself go completely to myself, as if it were a small inner camera in front of which to finally recite all my part. I had started in prison, and I had no longer stopped. In the loneliness of writing I found comfort and company and I discovered veins of my voice that I did not know. I felt safe in my intimacy, as if only there could be really at home. But life, you know, goes to steps, and one spring morning, suddenly, observing me in the mirror I had fearful. "What will happen to my diary when I will no longer be there?" I said to myself looking over for a moment. I am a woman who, albeit emotional and sometimes vulnerable, knows how to make her decisions. "I'm not there for anyone!" I said to Ines and Ninni and I retired to my room. I looked for that black notebook for a long time, with a rigid cover, which had been a companion of many thoughts, of many emotional ni. I leafed him slightly, without haste. Turning the pages, I felt the scent of the years, I felt my changes of mood from the differences of the graphic, now harsh, nervous, now more relaxed. Finally, I went to the bathroom and took a box of matches. A small gesture was enough and my words became fire, and then ashes. I had no regrets but only, at times, a little nostalgia. And I didn't stop writing. Since that time, however, every end of the year I rely on a match, as if it were the magical tool of a small ritual between me and me.

From my childhood I remember facts and situations very confused, but of a particular I have clear memory. I constantly kept a long bruise at the top of my forehead, just below the hairline. The cause of that sign was sleep: every night I followed the company to play small and big roles, daughters of kings, snotty children, protagonists of pitiful stories... Fact is that I had to stay awake until late. Often, as soon as I could, as I already told, I would lie down in a chest of costumes and take a nap. More than once my mother kept me asleep in her arms on her way home with the Balorda. But when I woke up in the morning to get ready to go to school, I was in debt of sleep to the point that, going to the bathroom dangling, I would sit on the toilet and after a moment, inevitably, I would fall forward with my head beating on the edge of the sink. That's why I was wearing bangs: to hide the bruise.

With the advent of the sound cinema (1929), my father, my uncle and all the company realize that the puppet theatre will soon be put in crisis, crushed by this new extraordinary and also a little magical means of show. To the great pain of Grandpa Pio, they decide a radical change of their program and their condition: "We will perform our shows, we will enter the stage, instead of puppets." Thus the two families of the Copper are replaced by wooden puppets (true and own articulated sculptures, three of which are still exhibited today at the Museo della Scala in Milan). They chose the texts, tried them and tried them again, they got the costumes and great backdrops painted for them by a stage designer friend of La Scala and they debut in a normal theater: the theater of "persona" and she, my mom, becomes the first actress. An actress who was raising her children during the day, helped them study, took care of the house, kept the administration of the company as if it were a normal family ménage. And in the evening, climbing the stage, here it is transformed into Juliet and Tosca, and the White Sister of the Sons of no one, and the Fantina of the Miserables, all roles that we daughters and cousins, one after the other, would then interpret. I see myself going through the theatrical apprenticeship by interpreting all the roles that growing up were suitable for my age, whether male or female.

Going back to the years of my very early childhood, when there was war, I convinced myself more and more than in the horror and difficulties of that period I met two things, two certainties that a child absolutely needs to live: protection and nourishment. It seems absurd to talk about each other when every day, now for now, we lived under bombs and massacres and we were hungry from morning to evening. But there, in Pozzuoli, where I was with my mother and my grandparents, every day there was a ritual that could satisfy those two needs of certainty. Each evening he expected the last train to pass, and then all the people of the country would pour into a long railway tunnel, where the next train would only pass at the next sunrise. We would go in there full of blankets and any rag that could keep us warm, form beds and go to sleep. A closed and heavy air, and so much frightened humanity. But in those hours under the tunnel you felt protected, and not only because you believed it to be bombproof, but because we were all there, next to each other, breathing the same air, taking courage, even finding the game and the joke. It seemed to me that I bought a huge family, my protector angels multiplied by ten, percent: safe and protected, with such a precise perception as never again would I have had in my life. Today I understand that that tunnel, deep down, was for me the peace of the unborn child in the womb.
